name: HMS Unruly (P 49)
events: The history of HMS P 49 / Unruly as compiled on this page is extracted the from patrol reports and logbooks of this submarine. Corrections and details regarding information from the enemy's side (for instance the composition of convoys attacked) is kindly provided by Mr. Platon Alexiades, a naval researcher from Canada.
This page was last updated in January 2022.

1 Nov 1942 Shortly before 1700 A/1, after having carried out compass adjustment trials, and a vacuum test earlier that day, P 49 (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) departed her builders yard at Barrow for Holy Loch. P 339 (Lt.Cdr. M.R.G. Wingfield, RN) was in company. They were escorted by HrMs Jan van Gelder (Lt.Cdr. P.L.M. van Geen, RNN). (1)
2 Nov 1942 At 1100 A/2, P 49 (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) arrived at Holy Loch to begin a period of trials and training.
At 1300 A/2, she departed for Gare Loch where acceptance trials were to be carried out the following day. (1)
3 Nov 1942 P 49 (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) conducted D/G trials off Helensburgh after which acceptance trials were carried out in Gare Loch.
P 49 then proceeded to Holy Loch where she arrived around 1730 A/3. She was then taken over from the shipyard and accepted for service in His Majesties Navy. (1)
5 Nov 1942 HMS P 49 (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) conducted exercises in the Clyde area. (1)
6 Nov 1942 HMS P 49 (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) conducted exercises in the Clyde area. These also included a deep dive trial. (1)
8 Nov 1942 HMS P 49 (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) proceeded from Holy Loch to Arrochar where she was to conduct her torpedo discharge trials on the torpedo firing range. (1)
10 Nov 1942 HMS P 49 (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) conducted exercises in Loch Long. (1)
11 Nov 1942 HMS P 49 (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) conducted torpedo discharge trials off Arrochar. (1)
12 Nov 1942 HMS P 49 (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) conducted torpedo discharge trials off Arrochar. Upon completion of these trials HMS P 49 returned to Holy Loch. (1)

13 Nov 1942 HMS P 49 (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) conducted gunnery and S.T.U. (mine detection) trials and D/F calibration trials in the Clyde area. These were followed by exercises which included night exercises. (1)
14 Nov 1942 HMS P 49 (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) conducted exercises in the Clyde area. Upon completion of these exercises she returned to Holy Loch. (1)
16 Nov 1942 HMS P 49 (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) conducted noise and speed trials in Loch Goil. The noise trials however had to be abandoned. (1)
17 Nov 1942 HMS P 49 (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) conducted noise trials in Loch Goil. (1)
18 Nov 1942 HMS P 49 (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) is docked at Kames Bay in AFD 7 to change her propellers. (1)
19 Nov 1942 HMS P 49 (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) is undocked and then performed speed trials in Loch Long. (1)
20 Nov 1942 HMS P 49 (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) conducted noise trials in Loch Goil. (1)
21 Nov 1942 HMS P 49 (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) conducted noise trials in Loch Goil. (1)

25 Nov 1942 HMS P 339 (Lt.Cdr. M.R.G. Wingfield, DSO, RN) and HMS P 49 (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) both conducted attack exercises in the Clyde area during which HrMs Jan van Gelder (Lt. P.L.M. van Geen, RNN) served as target. (2)

26 Nov 1942 During 26/27 November 1942, HMS P 339 (Lt.Cdr. M.R.G. Wingfield, DSO, RN) and HMS P 49 (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) conducted attack exercises in the Clyde area during which HrMs Jan van Gelder (Lt. P.L.M. van Geen, RNN) and HMS Breda (Capt.(Retd.) A.E. Johnston, RN) served as targets. These included night exercises. (2)
27 Nov 1942 HMS P 49 (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) proceeded to Campbeltown on completion of her night exercises. (1)

28 Nov 1942 HMS P 49 (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) conducted radar exercises off Campbeltown with HMS Breda (Capt.(Retd.) A.E. Johnston, RN). Upon completion of these exercises HMS P 49 proceeded to Rothesay. (1)
29 Nov 1942 HMS P 49 (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) conducted exercises in the Clyde area with HMS Breda (Capt.(Retd.) A.E. Johnston, RN). (1)
30 Nov 1942 HMS P 49 (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) conducted exercises in the Clyde area with HMS Breda (Capt.(Retd.) A.E. Johnston, RN). (1)
1 Dec 1942 HMS P 49 (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) conducted attack exercises in the Clyde area during which HMS Breda (Capt.(Retd.) A.E. Johnston, RN) served as target. (3)
2 Dec 1942 HMS P 49 (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) conducted attack exercises in the Clyde area during which HMS Breda (Capt.(Retd.) A.E. Johnston, RN) served as target. (3)
3 Dec 1942 HMS P 49 (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) conducted D/G trials off Helensburgh. (3)
4 Dec 1942 HMS P 49 (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) is docked at Kames Bay in AFD 7. (3)
5 Dec 1942 HMS P 49 (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) is undocked at Kames Bay and then returned to Holy Loch. (3)
7 Dec 1942 HMS P 49 (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) conducted D/G trials off Helensburgh. (3)

7 Dec 1942 Around 1900 A/7, HMS P 223 (Lt. G.D.N. Milner, DSC, RN) and HMS P 49 (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) both departed Holy Loch for Scapa Flow. They were escorted by HMS La Capricieuse (Lt.Cdr. G.W. Dobson, RNR). (4)
9 Dec 1942 Around 1145 A/9, HMS P 49 (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) arrived at Scapa Flow where she was to participate in A/S exercises. (3)

10 Dec 1942 HMS P 49 (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) conducted attack exercises off Scapa Flow during which HMS Anson ((Capt. H.R.G. Kinahan, CBE, RN, flying the flag of Vice-Admiral Sir B. Fraser, CB, KBE, RN) served as target. The battleship was escorted by HMS Inglefield (Cdr. A.G. West, RN), HMS Icarus (Lt.Cdr. E.N. Walmsley, DSC, RN) and HMS Eclipse (Lt.Cdr. E. Mack, DSO, DSC, RN).
While returning the her berth on completion of the exercises and on passing the boom, HMS P 49 fouled a propeller on the boom and drifted into a boom defence vessel sustaining damage. (3)
21 Dec 1942 HMS P 49 (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) is docked in AFD 12 at Scapa Flow. (3)
22 Dec 1942 HMS P 49 (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) is undocked at Scapa Flow. She departed for Lerwick later the same day around 1700 A/22 escorted by HMS Cape Barracouta (T/Skr. R.C. Heather, RNR). (5)
23 Dec 1942 Around 0920 A/23, HMS P 49 (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) arrived at Lerwick from Scapa Flow.
Around 1600 A/23, she departed Lerwick for her 1st war patrol later the same day. She was to patrol off northern Norway to provide cover for convoys JW 51 / RA 51.

She was to proceed through position 'A' (090° - Out Skerries - 5 miles), then via route Orange to position 71°25'N, 18°15'E, thence to a wait-position in 71°25'N, 22°00'E. P 49 was to leave her waiting position at 1800 A/28 for patrol position 'R 3'.
For the daily positions of HMS P 49 during this patrol see the map below.
25 Dec 1942 At 1533 A/25, the Officer of the Watch, dived HMS P 49 (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) in position 65°14'N, 05°32'E, after a lookout reported seeing a submarine diving 1.5 miles away.

The Officer of the Watch did not see this submarine himself but the lookout was certain he was not mistaken. Course of the submarine was estimated by him as being 340°. P 49 went to 100 feet but no HE was heard.
At 1555 A/25, HMS P 49 went to periscope depth but nothing was sighted.
At 1625 A/25, HMS P 49 surfaced after dusk and continued her passage northwards.
[No enemy submarine was in the area though as was no other Allied submarine. The lookout was thus most likely mistaken after all.] (7)

3 Jan 1943 In a signal timed 1641 A/3, the Capt. S.9 sent the following orders to the submarines mentioned; HMS Graph (Lt. P.B. Marriott, RN), HMS P 49 (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN), HMS P 312 (Lt.Cdr. R.M. Favell, RN) and HMS P 216 (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) leave patrol 1600 A/4th for Lerwick passing through following positions; A) Graph, 70°22'N, 12°45'E, thence by route 'Pink'. B) P 49, 70°54'N, 16°40'E, thence by route 'Orange'. C) P 312, 71°10'N, 16°02'E, thence by route 'Black'. D) P 216, 71°26'N, 17°00'E, thence by route 'Black'. (8)
4 Jan 1943 At 1600 A/4, HMS P 49 (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) surfaced in patrol position R 3 and set course for position 70°54'N, 16°40'E. (7)

5 Jan 1943 In a signal timed 1814 A/5, the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) sent the following orders to HMS Graph (Lt. P.B. Marriott, RN), HMS P 49 (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN), HMS P 312 (Lt.Cdr. R.M. Favell, RN) and HMS P 216 (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN); After crossing latitude 68°00'N submarines are to proceed dived by day as follows; Graph to the vicinity of position 64°35'N, 09°44'E. P 312 to the vicinity of position 64°44'N, 09°26'E. This was later corrected to 64°55'N, 09°56'E in the F.O.S.'s signal timed 1651 A/6. P 49 to the vicinity of position 66°42'N, 11°56'E. P 216 to the vicinity of position 66°56'N, 12°01'E.
The Admiralty expected the German battleship Tirpitz to proceed from Trondheim to Narvik. (8)

6 Jan 1943 In a signal timed 1134 A/6, the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) sent the following orders to HMS Graph (Lt. P.B. Marriott, RN), HMS P 49 (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN), HMS P 312 (Lt.Cdr. R.M. Favell, RN) and HMS P 216 (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN); 1) I appreciate that enemy main units may be moving to the Norwegian coast shortly. 2) Object of submarines on patrol off the Norwegian coast are cruiser(s) and above but submarines except P 312 may attack U-Boats if definitely identified as hostile. One full salvo is to be retained for main units. 3) If force retires at any time S/Ms have freedom of action as follows; P 216 north and P 49 south of 66°50'N, P 312 north and Graph south of 64°45'N. 4) Landfalls have been satisfactorily made in past as follows; (A) Röst, (B) Myken, (C) Sklindenbank, (D) Nordoerne. 5) Submarines are not to enter following areas unless ordered by F.O.S. 6) Submerged bombing restrictions have been arranged in patrol areas until further notice.

In a signal timed 1727 A/6, these submarines were informed that they were not to be on the surface within 30 miles from position 64°45'N, 09°49'E from 0800 A/7 until further notice and within 30 miles from position 66°50'N, 11°57'E from 0100 A/7 until further notice. (8)

13 Jan 1943 In a signal timed 1321 A/13, the Capt. S.9 sent the following orders to HMS P 49 (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN), HMS P 312 (Lt.Cdr. R.M. Favell, RN and HMS P 216 (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN); 1) P 49, P 312, P 216 leave patrol for Lerwick forthwith routed as follows. 2) P 49 through position 65°30'N, 05°50'E thence by route 'Orange'. 3) P 312 through position 63°20'N, 02°50'E thence by route 'Orange'. 4) P 216 through positions 63°00'N, 03°10'E and 090° - Muckle Flugga - 10 miles. 5) Diving by day until 60 miles clear of the Norwegian coast thence on the surface at Commanding Officers discretion. (7)
16 Jan 1943 Around 0930 A/16, HMS P 49 (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) ended her 1st war patrol at Lerwick.

Around 1300 A/16, she departed Lerwick for Holy Loch. She made the passage together with HMS P 312 (Lt.Cdr. R.M. Favell, RN), HMS P 55 (Lt. A.D. Piper, DSC and 2 bars, RNR). They were escorted by HMS Cutty Sark (Cdr.(Retd.) R.H. Mack, RN). (7)
18 Jan 1943 At 1535 A/18, HMS P 49 (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) arrived at Holy Loch from Lerwick. (9)
21 Jan 1943 HMS P 49 (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) is docked in No.2 graving dock at Govan. (9)
26 Jan 1943 HMS P 49 (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) is undocked at Govan and returned to Holy Loch. (9)
27 Jan 1943 HMS P 49 (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) proceeded from Holy Loch to Loch Goil where noise trials were to be carried out. (9)
28 Jan 1943 HMS P 49 (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) conducted noise trials in Loch Goil. She returned to Holy Loch on completion.
[No logs are available for February to August 1943, so no further details can be given for the moment other then the ones currently listed. Some details for this period will thus be missing.] (9)
5 Feb 1943 HMS P 49 (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) went aground at Holy Loch during a gale.
She was scheduled to leave the U.K. for Gibraltar the following day. (10)
15 Feb 1943 The sailing of HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) for the Mediterranean was delayed further due to defects. She was taken in hand for repairs on the 21st. (10)
7 Mar 1943 The completion of the repairs to HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) was delayed for another three days due to salt water in the battery tanks. (10)
11 Mar 1943 HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) finally completed her repairs at Glasgow and returned to Holy Loch. (11)

14 Mar 1943 At 1930 A/14, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) departed Holy Loch for Gibraltar. During passage south through the Irish Sea she was escorted by HMS Cutty Sark (Cdr.(Retd.) R.H. Mack, RN) until position 000° - Round Island - 7 miles.
HMS Unruly was then to proceed via route 'Red' to position 43°01'N, 12°26'W. Further routing would be signalled by the Vice-Admiral Gibraltar.
[As no log is available for this period no map can be displayed.] (10)

20 Mar 1943 In a signal timed 0120 A/20, the Flag Officer Gibraltar sent the following orders to HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN); 1) After crossing 43°01'N at 0500 A/22 pass through the following positions at time stated; Position 'T' = 36°51'N, 09°13'E at 0235 A/26. Position 'U' = 36°37'N, 07°24'E at 0045 A/27. Position 'V' = 35°53'N, 06°15'E at 0930 A/27. Position 'W' = 35°49'N, 18°06.5'E at 1020 A/27 thence to Gibraltar by Q.K.A. 87 (swept channel through a mined area). 2) Speed of advance diving by day 103 miles per 24 hours. After passing position 'U' proceedeed on surface at your discretion at 7.5 knots. Do not cross meridian 06°40'W before 0700 A/27 but thereafter increase speed to 10 knots. 3) Submerged bombing restrictions have been arranged until 0045 A/27 thereafter total bombing restrictions 10 miles either side of your route from 35 miles ahead to 35 miles astern of your estimated position. Report if you are not able to keep your bombing restrictions. (12)

22 Mar 1943 In a signal timed 1237 A/22, the Flag Officer Gibraltar, informed HMS Ultor (Lt. G.E. Hunt, DSC, RN), ORP Dzik (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) B.S. Romanowski), HMS Shakespeare (Lt. M.F.R. Ainslie, DSC, RN) and HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) of the following; HMS Nasturtium (Lt. C.D. Smith, DSC, RNR) and one tug sailed at 1300 A/22 routed through position 225° - Cape St. Vincent - 5 miles to the vicinity of Lisbon at 12 knots.

In a signal timed 1921 A/22, the Flag Officer Gibraltar, the above listed submarines were informed of the following; HMS Haarlem (T/Lt. J.R.T. Broom, RNVR) sailed 1930 A/22 at 8 knots on the same route as in my signal timed 1237 A/22. (12)

24 Mar 1943 In a signal timed 0050 A/24, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) informed the Flag Officer Gibraltar of the following; Estimate position at 0700 A/24th is 40°09'N, 10°56'W, thereafter speed of advance 105 miles per day. E.T.A. position 'U' [36°37'N, 07°24'E], 0500 A/27. From this time a speed of 10 knots can he maintained. [A weather report was also included in the signal].
The Flag Officer Gibraltar responded as follows in his signal timed 1846 A/24; Your 0050 A/24 approved. Bombing restrictions adjusted.

In a signal timed 2146 A/24, the Flag Officer Gibraltar informed HMS Unruly of the following; HMS Vanoc (A/Cdr. C.F.H. Churchill, RN), HMS Nasturtium (Lt. C.D. Smith, DSC, RNR) and HMS Haarlem (T/Lt. J.R.T. Broom, RNVR) are patrolling off Lisbon awaiting three merchant vessel for escort coastwise to Gibraltar at 8 knots. (13)

25 Mar 1943 In a signal timed 2301 A/25 and amended in his signal timed 1542 A/26 informed HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) of the following; HMS Vanoc (A/Cdr. C.F.H. Churchill, RN) and HMS Nasturtium (Lt. C.D. Smith, DSC, RNR) with two merchant ships sailed from Lisbon at 2000 A/25 coast wise to Gibraltar at 9.5 knots. HMS St. Nectan (T/A/Lt.Cdr. T.F. Broadhead, RNR) and one merchant ship coast wise to Lisbon will be passing Meridian 08°00'W. at 1800 A/26 speed 9.5 knots. (13)
27 Mar 1943 HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) arrived at Gibraltar (most likely in the afternoon). (14)

5 Apr 1943 At 1430 A/5, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) departed Gibraltar for her 2nd war patrol (1st in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol near the Spanish / French border, Port Vendres area.

The Flag Officer Gibraltar routed her as follows; Position 'S' = 180° - Gape Calaburras - 5 miles at 1930 A/5. Position 'T' = 180° - Cape de Gata - 5 miles at 2100 A/6 And unless otherwise ordered by Capt. S.8, position 'U' = 135° - Cape Palos - 5 miles at 1330 A/6.
At 2100 A/6, the Capt. S.8 would take operation control.
The Capt. S.8 ordered the further routing of Unruly as follows; Positions 40°01'N, 01°51'E at 0300 A/9 and 42°19'N, 03°57'E at 2200 A/10.
[As no log is available for this period, no map can be displayed.] (7)

6 Apr 1943 In a signal timed 1724 A/6, the Flag Officer Gibraltar informed HMS Tactician (Lt.Cdr. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN), HMS Ultor (Lt. G.E. Hunt, DSC, RN), HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) and ORP Dzik (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) B.S. Romanowski) of the following; 1) The Spanish merchant vessel Manuchu (3119 GRT, built 1921) is expected to leave Puerto Barrios vicinity Valencia P.M./5 5th for Marseilles. 2) The Spanish merchant vessel Ciano (4264 GRT, built 1901) leaves Genoa on the 9th by the approved route for Gibraltar. 3) The Spanish merchant vessel (Swiss chartered) Briquetas Zorroza (1730 GRT, built 1889) now leaves Genoa on the 7th for Gibraltar. (15)

8 Apr 1943 In a signal timed 1054 A/8, the Flag Officer Gibraltar informed HMS Tactician (Lt.Cdr. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN), HMS Ultor (Lt. G.E. Hunt, DSC, RN), HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) and ORP Dzik (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) B.S. Romanowski) of the following; The German transport Lezardrieux (former French, 865 GRT, built 1922) and Danish transport (under German control) Saint Lucien [Actually renamed Aalborg at this time] (former French, 1256 GRT, built 1920) arrived at Valencia on the 7th from Marseilles in ballast. (15)

9 Apr 1943 In a signal timed 0353 A/9, the Flag Officer Gibraltar informed HMS Tactician (Lt.Cdr. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN), HMS Ultor (Lt. G.E. Hunt, DSC, RN), HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) and ORP Dzik (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) B.S. Romanowski) of the following; Saint Lucien [Actually renamed Aalborg at this time] (former French, 1256 GRT, built 1920) will sail tonight 8th from Burriana for Marseilles. (15)

10 Apr 1943 In a signal timed 2121 A/10, the Flag Officer Gibraltar informed HMS Ultor (Lt. G.E. Hunt, DSC, RN), HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) and ORP Dzik (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) B.S. Romanowski) of the following; The German transport Lezardrieux (former French, 865 GRT, built 1922) will sail from Valencia tonight 10th for Marseilles / Orange. (15)

11 Apr 1943 HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) fires two torpedoes at a small Spanish merchant vessel [so far not identified] off Cape Bear near Port Vendres, southern France. Both torpedoes missed their target.
0134 A/11 - Entered the patrol area.
0516 A/11 - Dived off Cape Bear in the hope of intercepting the transport Lezardrieux (former French, 865 GRT, built 1922)

1218 A/11 - Sighted a southbound merchant vessel of about 1000 tons. She was flying no flag, but had Spanish colours painted on her bow, stern and amidships. Started attack. Enemy course was 150°, speed 9 knots.
1245 A/11 - Identified the funnel markings of the merchant vessel as belonging to the Bachi Shipping Company.

1248 A/11 - Fired two torpedoes from 1200 yards. No hits were obtained and the torpedoes were heard to explode on hitting land. It is thought the torpedoes ran under the target which was in ballast. Retired to the eastward.
1430 A/11 - As all was still quiet returned to the vicinity of Cape Bear to intercept the expected Lezardrieux. (7)
12 Apr 1943 HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) torpedoed and sank the Danish merchant (in German control) Aalborg (1256 GRT, built 1920) off Cape Bear (near Port Vendres, southern France).
1305 A/12 - Sighted a motor launch bearing 300° about 3 miles away.
1309 A/12 - Two depth charges were dropped apparently by the motor launch.

1425 A/12 - Sighted a northbound merchant vessel of about 1000 tons. Closed to attack. Enemy course was 333°, speed 8.5 knots. 1445 A/12 - Fired two torpedoes from 2000 yards. Two torpedo explosions were heard.
1448 A/12 - Asdic picked up breaking up noises. HE had ceased.
1449 A/12 - At periscope depth. Saw the target to be down by the bow but still upright. Went to 80 feet and retired from the area.
1509 A/12 - Returned to periscope depth. The target was not sighted.
1510 A/12 - Sighted the motor launch bearing 270° at a range of 2 miles. Between now and 1530 hours approximately 20 depth charges were dropped about 2 miles to the southward. (7)

12 Apr 1943 In a signal timed 1704 A/12 the Capt. S.8 sent the following orders to HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) and HMS Ultor (Lt. G.E. Hunt, DSC, RN); Leave patrol as follows; Unruly through positions 42°19'N, 04°34'E at 0230 A/16, 39°54'N, 04°38'E at 1500 A/17 and 38°14'N, 03°18'E at 2030 A/18. Ultor through position 42°19'N, 06°25'E at 0100 A/16, 37°58'N, 04°15'E at 2030 A/18. Thence both submarines proceeding on the surface at the Commanding Officers discretion to make rendezvous with the escort to Algiers in position 36°57'N, 03°12'E at 0615 A/19. (16)

14 Apr 1943 At 0802 A/14, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) sighted a southbound merchant vessel in ballast. The ship had a black hull with the Spanish flag and markings and a black funnel. A dummy attack was carried out on this vessel.
The ship passed about 2 miles to the southward of the Fort de Brescou. Another ship was on her starboard beam at 2000 yards.
At 0835 A/14, after having completed the dummy attack HMS Unruly had to deep for an aircraft which is thought to be the air escort. Ten minutes later HMS Unruly returned to periscope depth. (7)
16 Apr 1943 At 0225 A/16, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) left her patrol area to proceed to Algiers. (7)
19 Apr 1943 Around 0745 A/19, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) ended her 2nd war patrol (1st in the Mediterranean) at Algiers.
She, and HMS Ultor (Lt. G.E. Hunt, DSC, RN), were escorted in by HMS Juliet (T/Lt. T. Paxton, RNR) which had joined around 0600 A/19. (7)
24 Apr 1943 Around 1900 A/24, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) departed Algiers for Malta.

She was ordered to proceed through positions 38°15'N, 03°04'E at 0400 A/25, 38°15'N, 11°19'E at 0300 A/29 thence through position 'P' to Malta as will be signalled by the Capt. S.10. [We have, so far, been unable to find the exact coordinates of position 'P'.]
For the daily positions of HMS Unruly during this passage see the map below.

27 Apr 1943 In a signal timed 1029 B/27, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 49 (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN); Pass position 'P' at 0600 B/29th and proceed through positions 'Q' and 'R' to arrive at the end of the Malta searched channel at 0800 B/1. (17)

28 Apr 1943 At 1930 A/28, in position 38°13'N, 10°18'E, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) picked up HE bearing 215° doing 360 revolutions. The HE from a diesel engine and is thought to be a submarine. The light was beginning to fade and nothing could be seen through the periscope. It was however considered not dark enough to surface, particularly because the target would be against the darker horizon. The HE passed slowly up the starboard side.
At 1954 A/28, course was altered to 145° to bring the bearing of the HE right ahead and then surfaced. On surfacing a silhouette was seen momentarily right ahead but it could not be identified.
At 2010 A/28, the HE was lost. (7)
29 Apr 1943 At 0415 A/24, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) dived for a motor torpedo boat in position 37°59'N, 11°22'E.
At 1215 A/24, in position 37°41'N, 11°22'E, a Spica-class torpedo boat was sighted bearing 270° proceeding on a course of 045°. (7)
1 May 1943 Shortly after 1200 A/1, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) arrived at Malta from Algiers. At Malta she joined the 10th Submarine Flotilla. (7)
12 May 1943 At 1500 B/12, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) departed Malta for her 3rd war patrol (2nd in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol east of Sicily.
On departure speed runs were carried out on the measured mile.
For the daily and attack positions of HMS Unruly during this patrol see the map below.

15 May 1943 At 0745 A/15, near Cape Moline to the north of Catania, Sicily, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) sighted a small northbound merchant vessel at a range of 6000 yards. Unfortunately the visibility was patchy and the ship was not sighted until it had passed so an attack was no longer possible. (7)

16 May 1943 HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) torpedoed and damaged the Italian transport Nicolo Tommaseo (4573 GRT, built 1943) about 5 nautical miles north-north-east of Catania, Sicily, Italy in position 37°35'N, 15°17'E.

Nicolo Tommaseo was escorted by the torpedo boat Clio. They had sailed from Catania at 0947 B/16 for Messina at a speed of 10 knots. The freighter was hit in the stern section and Clio reported to have been missed by two torpedoes as well, and, immediately proceeded to attack the submarine. A passing naval ferry barge (MZ type) was sighted and was ordered to pick up the wounded from the stricken ship and land them at Catania while Clio asked the assistance of a tug. The torpedo-boat kept on hunting the submarine, dropping a total of ten 100-kg depth charges until 1550 B/16 when the corvettes Driade and Euterpe arrived on the scene and took over the A/S search. Clio then took Nicolo Tommaseo in tow as no tug had yet showed up. Two hours later the tug Littorio arrived and took the freighter in tow back to Catania, the escort being entrusted to the two corvettes while Clio proceeded alone to Messina.
The damaged Nicolo Tommaseo was sunk by Allied aircraft at Catania on 26 July 1943.
1010 B/16 - Sighted a northbound merchant vessel escorted by a Spica-class destroyer and one aircraft. Started attack.
1018 B/16 - The target zigged towards.
1024 B/16 - The target again zigged towards.
1032 B/16 - Fired four torpedoes from 1500 yards. One hit was obtained.
1042 B/16 - Depth charging started.
1058 B/16 - The last depth charge was dropped. A total of 10 had been dropped but HMS Unruly was not damaged.
1126 B/16 - Returned to periscope depth. Sighted four aircraft patrolling the area. The merchant vessel was still in the area. Went deep to reload a torpedo tube.
1214 B/16 - Having reloaded no.1 tube, returned to periscope depth.
1215 B/16 - Near missed by two small bombs from an aircraft. Retired to 80 feet.

1235 B/16 - Two more bombs were dropped close. Went to 130 feet. Six more bombs were dropped were dropped singly. Retired to the south-eastward at 130 feet. Carried on reloading which was completed at 1430 hours.

2122 B/16 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1944 B/16 which contained the following; One merchant ship towed by a destroyer escorted by another in position 070° - Catania - 7 miles, course N, speed 4 knots, at 1700 B/16. Air strikes will take place at 2245 B/16. (7)
17 May 1943 Between 1600 B/17 and 1700 B/17, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) sighted six southbound caiques close inshore off Cape Campolato (just north of Augusta). (7)
18 May 1943 At 1845 B/18, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) sighted a merchant vessel at anchor in Catania harbour.
At 2113 B/18, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1901 B/18 was received. [For the text of this signal, see the other event for 18 May 1943.] (7)

18 May 1943 In a signal timed 1901 B/18, the Capt. S.10 sent the following information to HMS Ultor (Lt. G.E. Hunt, DSC, RN) and HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN); At 1600 B/18, one merchant ship of about 3000 tons, escorted by one destroyer and three motor torpedo boats sighted in position 021° - Point Alice - 4 miles southbound, 8 knots. An air strike may be expected on this convoy. (18)
19 May 1943 At 1435 B/19, near Cape Mulini, just north of Catania, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) sighted a southbound landing craft just outside the 100 fathom line.

At 1930 B/19, two small northbound 'destroyers' were sighted. They were in no hurry and frequently one or the other turned round or stopped to investigate imaginary A/S contacts. Lt. Fyfe considered that the presence of HMS Unruly was suspected. It was considered that the two 'destroyers' were probably going to meet the ship reported in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1901 B/18 and escort her into Catania after dark. (7)
20 May 1943 At 1520 B/20, near Cape Molini, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) sighted an auxiliary schooner, under power, close inshore. (7)

21 May 1943 Shortly before midnight, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) attacked the Italian submarine Marcantonio Bragadin with a full salvo off torpedoes to the east of Augusta, Sicily. No hits were obtained.
The Italian submarine was on passage from Lampedusa to Taranto having made a storage trip. The four torpedo tracks were sighted just in time and the submarine turned away.
0103 B/21 - Sighted the Cape Santa Croce lighthouse (off Augusta) burning at full brilliancy. Closed at speed as something may be approaching Augusta.
0115 B/21 - The lighthouse was extinguished for 30 minutes.

0240 B/21 - When three miles to the north-eastward of Cape Santa Croce course was altered to the southward with the intention of patrolling approximately one mile to the north of the usual approach route to Augusta.
0317 B/21 - Two more lighthouses on the approach route to Augusta came to full brilliancy.
0324 B/21 - Sighted, what was thought to be a submarine bearing 095°.

0328 B/21 - Dived hoping to be able to attack from periscope depth. On diving the target was seen through the periscope but also turbine HE was reported. This HE closed on a steady bearing. The target could not be identified through the periscope.
0336 B/21 - The target was seen to fire a recognition glare of one red and three white stars.
0340 B/21 - The target was now very close.
0345 B/21 - Went deep. The HE passed very close ahead and finally faded out bearing Red 70°. It was not heard or seen again.
Between 0956 B/21 and 1000 B/21 - Twenty-two distant explosions were heard.
1040 B/21 - Head a single distant explosion followed by another one eight minutes later.
1333 B/21 - Sighted three motor torpedo boats entering Augusta.

1640 B/21 - Closed the harbour entrance of Augusta hoping to fire through the harbour entrance at a small tanker seen anchored inside the harbour. However later a boom was sighted and the idea was abandoned.
2250 B/21 - Sighted the Cape Santa Croce light.
2252 B/21 - Sighted the Cape Murro di Porco light. Proceeded northwards on an approach course towards Augusta.
2344 B/21 - Sighted an outward bound submarine on the port quarter.
2345 B/21 - Dived. Started attack.
2357 B/21 - In position 37°12'N, 15°30'E fired a salvo of four torpedoes from 3000 yards. No hits were obtained.
0022 B/22 - Surfaced and set course for Malta as all torpedoes has been expended. (7)

21 May 1943 In a signal timed 1113 B/21, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to the submarines mentioned; HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) leave patrol to return to Malta and arrive at the end of the searched channel at 0830 B/24 by the reverse of the outward route. HMS Ultor (Lt. G.E. Hunt, DSC, RN) is to shift to area 'T'. ORP Dzik (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) B.S. Romanowski) is to shift to area 'M' at 0600 B/22. (19)
24 May 1943 At 1000 B/24, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) ended her 3rd war patrol (2nd in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (7)
4 Jun 1943 HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) departed Malta for her 4th war patrol (3rd in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol north of Sicily.
For the daily and attack positions during this patrol see the map below.
After passing position 'P' on clearing Q.B.B. 255 (mined area) she was to proceed through position 38°30'N, 11°35'E thence through zones 'A', 'B' and 'C' to patrol in zone 'F'.

9 Jun 1943 During the day, while patrolling near Cape Vaticano, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) sighted seven large schooners. Four southbound and three northbound. Also a large number of transport aircraft were sighted. These were mainly JU 52's. The southbound ones appeared to be laden wile the others appeared to be empty. (7)

10 Jun 1943 At 0105 B/10, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1115 B/9 regarding the effectiveness of the Cape Vaticano radar station. It was suggested to patrol between Cape Suvero and Cape Bonifati instead.
At 0434 B/10, HMS Unruly dived to the north of Cape Vaticano and then followed the 100 fathom line up towards Cape Suvero.
During the day three schooners were seen. Also a total of 24 JU 52 tranport aircraft were counted passing through the area.

At 2154 B/10, the Capt. S.8's signal timed 2040 B/10 was received in with it was reported that the enemy had detected a submarine near Cape Orlando, Sicily. This was however not HMS Unruly as was considered possible by the Capt. S.8.
At 2250 B/10, starshell and gunflashes were seen off the north coast of Sicily. (20)
11 Jun 1943 At 1405 A/11, near Paola, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) sighted five southbound landing craft proceeding close inshore. These were heavily laden.
[F 150, F 466, TK 502, TK 505 and TK 507 on passage from Naples via Scario to Palermo and Catania, roll 2574 KTB Seetransportchef Italien] (7)
12 Jun 1943 HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) attacked a southbound merchant vessel with four torpedoes west of Paola, Italy. No hits were obtained.
[so far, as if December 2018, the target has not been identified.]
0032 B/12 - Sighted a southbound merchant vessel of about 3000 tons. A trawler was seen on her port quarter. Started surface attack.
0051 B/12 - In position 39°22'N, 15°56'E fired four torpedoes from 2000 yards. Dived on firing. No hits were obtained.
0115 B/12 - Commenced reload.
0937 B/12 - Sighted a small southbound A/S trawler, close inshore.

At 2206 B/12, the Capt. S.8's signal timed 1350 B/12 was received which contained the following; Leave patrol after dark/14 through positions 39°01'N, 14°59'E and 39°01'N, 12°25'E thence through position 'P' at 2230 A/16 to Malta as will be ordered by the Capt. S.10. (21)

13 Jun 1943 HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) only sighted one small schooner proceeding close inshore near Paola. This vessel was only about 80 tons and was not considered worth attacking with the deck gun while close to the land.
At 2108 B/14, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1103 B/13 was received which contained the route to Malta from position 'P'. [We have been unable to find the text of this signal.] (21)

14 Jun 1943 HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) torpedoed and sank the Italian transport Valentino Coda (4485 GRT, built 1924) 19 nautical miles bearing 309° of Cape Vaticano, Italy in position 38°52'N, 15°27'E.

The Valentino Coda was proceeding from Naples to Messina escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Animoso. She was hit by two torpedoes and sank by the stern, the torpedo boat hunted the submarine four four and a half hours and returned to pick up the survivors who had taken to three lifeboats, three men were missing.
0140 B/14 - Sighted a merchant vessel of about 5000 tons escorted by 'what is identified as' a destroyer. Range was 10000 yards. Started attack.
0200 B/14 - Fired four torpedoes from a range of 3500 yards. About two and a half minutes later two explosions were heard.
0204 B/14 - Dived. Ten minutes later six depth charges were dropped but these were a long way off.
2124 B/14 - Surfaced and set course to return to Malta in accordance with the orders. (7)

15 Jun 1943 At 2117 A/15, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1016 B/15 which contained the following orders; Cancel my 1103 B/13. Onward route from position 'P' through position 37°31'N, 12°27'E, thence through position 'V', thence direct for the Malta West Sanctuary rendezvous to arrive there at 0850 B/18. (21)
18 Jun 1943 At 1000 B/18, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) ended her 4th war patrol (3rd in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She, and HMS Tactician (Lt.Cdr. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN), were escorted in by HMS Rye (Lt. J.A. Pearson, DSC and Bar, RNR). (7)
1 Jul 1943 At 1830 B/1, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) departed Malta for her 5th war patrol (4th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol north of the Straits of Messina.
She is routed through positions 36°08'N, 14°22'E, 37°03'N, 11°49'E and 37°48'N, 11°54'E, thence rounding Marettimo Island and proceeding north of Stromboli to the patrol area.
6 Jul 1943 At 0130 B/6, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) entered her patrol area.
At 1125 B/6, near Cape Vaticano, HMS Unruly sighted a southbound Spica-class torpedo boat.
At 1503 B/6, a small northbound tug was seen close inshore.
A few schooners and transport aircraft were seen throughout the day. (7)
7 Jul 1943 At 0840 A/7, near Cape Vaticano, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) sighted two southbound coasters.
At 1020 A/7, a southbound tug was sighted.

At 1125 A/7, a southbound merchant vessel and escort were seen. Visibility was patchy and the ships were lost from sight around ten minutes later. It is thought they had altered course to the westward. (7)

10 Jul 1943 At 0300 B/10, near Cape Vaticano, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) sighted three darkened ships and closed to identify. The ships were subsequently identified as a merchant ship in ballast escorted by two destroyers. They were northbound. The merchant ship was of about 3000 tons.
No attack was made as only loaded southbound ships were to be attacked according to the patrol orders.
Lt. Fyfe turned stern on to the nearest escort.
At 0310 B/10, a destroyer was seen to turn towards. HMS Unruly dived.
By 0337 B/10, all was clear and no more HE could be heard so HMS Unruly surfaced. (7)

11 Jul 1943 HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) attacked a southbound submarine near Cape Vaticano. No hits were obtained. Shortly before another southbound submarine had already been sighted but no attack could be made due to the range being too great.
0515 B/11 - HE was picked up on bearing 120°. It was later seen this originated from three motor torpedo boats.
0737 B/11 - Picked up HE bearing 120° but sighted nothing.
1048 B/11 - A northbound schooner was seen close inshore and a southbound one to seaward.

1435 B/11 - Sighted a southbound Italian submarine near Cape Vaticano. Range was 6000 yards and she was unable to attack. This was most likely the Italian Flutto which had left Bonifacio the day before and was proceeding to her patrol area off Catania through the Straits of Messina.
1550 B/11 – Sighted a second southbound submarine thought to be a German u-boat. Started attack. enemy course was 160°, speed 14 knots.
1605 B/11 – Fired four torpedoes from 3000 yards. No hits were obtained as the enemy was seen to alter course after the torpedoes had been fired. Position was approximately 38°36'N, 15°40'E.
This was actually the Italian Bronzo. She had sailed from Pozzuoli the day before and was proceeding to her patrol area off Syracuse.

2333 B/11 - Sighted three darkened ships towards Cape Vaticano. Increased to full surface speed to close and identify. A radar range of 9000 yards was obtained. The ships were identified as a large northbound merchant vessel and a large tanker escorted by a destroyer which was stationed ahead. Broke off the attack as it was only allowed to attack southbound laden merchant vessels.
2346 B/11 - The destroyer fired three well placed star shell round over the submarine. Dived.
0014 B/12 - All clear. No more HE was heard. Surfaced. (7)
12 Jul 1943 At 0625 B/12, near Cape Vaticano, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) sighted a northbound merchant vessel of about 1000 tons proceeding close inshore.
At 1226 B/12, a southbound schooner was sighted.

At 2002 B/12, two southbound German submarines escorted by a trawler were sighted. A dummy attack was carried out from a range of 1000 yards. Fire was withheld as a full salvo was to be retained for cruisers and above.
At 2215 B/12, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1725 B/12 was received in which the restrictions on torpedo fire were lifted.
During the day no less then around 300 transport aircraft were sighted throughout the day. These were mainly southbound. (7)

13 Jul 1943 HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) attacked the Italian submarine Benedetto Brin off Cape Vaticano near Messina, Sicily, Italy in position 38°35'N, 15°49'E. The Brin had sailed from Bonifacio the day before and after initially ordered to a patrol area north of the Galite Islands, she was then ordered to an area north of Cape San Vito (Sicily) and then again switched to an area between Catania and Syracuse. The British were under the impression that the submarine had been sunk. In fact the submarine Acciaio is often listed as being sunk by HMS Unruly but this was not the case as her target was clearly the Brin which escaped without damage.
1150 B/13 - Sighted two southbound schooners.
1442 B/13 - Sighted a northbound hospital ship on which a dummy attack was carried out from a range of 800 yards.
2022 B/13 - Sighted a southbound Italian submarine. Started attack.

2049 B/13 - Fired four torpedoes from 3000 yards. The first torpedo hit the target two minutes after firing. The other three torpedoes hit the coast and exploded. Actually, all missed as Brin sighted the submerging British submarine at a distance of 1500 metres and took avoiding action.
2055 B/13 - Turbine HE was reported to be closing on bearing 055°. Went to 80 feet and altered course to the east.
2137 B/13 - Picked up diesel HE bearing 185°, closing. It passed very close astern and faded bearing 025°. Nothing was seen though as it was very dark by now.
2334 B/13 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1247 B/13. [For the text of this signal see the other 'event' for 13 July 1943.] (7)

13 Jul 1943 In a signal timed 1247 B/13, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to the submarines mentioned; Leave patrol by reverse of outward route to pass through position 38°18'N, 11°30'E at following time which are to be strictly adhered to. ORP Sokol (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) J.K.S. Koziolkowski, DSC), 0001 B/18. HMS Ultor (Lt. G.E. Hunt, DSC, RN), 0200 B/18. HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN), 0400 B/18. Thence through positions 38°10'N, 10°25'E, 37°44'N, 09°33'E to make rendezvous in position 37°26'N, 09°33'E at 1800 B/19. The Naval Officer In Commmand (N.O.I.C.) Bizerta is requested to provide escort in the rendezvous position at 1200 B/19 flying a large black flag at the masthead and to the suspend A/S.V. air search in the area. (22)
14 Jul 1943 At 0240 B/14, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) sighted a destroyer coming towards. Lt. Fyfe order the submarine to dive to evade.
At 1455 B/14, a merchant vessel of about 5000 tons, escorted by a destroyer, was seen coming from the south of Stromboli and steering towards Cape Vaticano. A dummy attack was carried out.
At 1523 B/14, four loud explosions at 7 second intervals were heard. They were like torpedo explosions. It was thought another Allied submarine had attacked the convoy but this is not the case.
At 1532 B/14, the destroyer dropped four depth charges to the westward.
At 1916 B/14, off Cape Vaticano, a southbound enemy submarine was sighted north of the Messina Strait. Four minutes later an enemy report was passed to HMS Ultor (Lt. G.E. Hunt, DSC, RN).
[Perhaps Alagi or Dandolo ???] (7)
15 Jul 1943 At 0130 B/15, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) left her patrol area to proceed to Bizerta as had been ordered.
In a signal timed 0335 B/14, the Capt. S.10 had ordered to leave patrol to comply with his 1247 B/13 subtracting 24 hours. An escort into Bizerta would be supplied. (23)
18 Jul 1943 Around 1715 B/18, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) grounded off Point de la Carriere, Bizerta Tunisia. Apparently she quickly got off and proceeded into Bizerta.
She was being escorted in by HMS Kintyre (T/Lt. C. Richardson, RNVR) which had joined around 1400 B/18. (7)

23 Jul 1943 Around 1200 B/23, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) departed Bizerta for passage to Malta. She made the passage together with HMS Ultor (Lt. G.E. Hunt, DSC, RN) and ORP Sokol (Lt.Cdr. J.C. Koziolkowski, ORP). They were escorted by HMS Bude (Lt. F.A.J. Andrew, RN) and HMS Clacton (A/Lt.Cdr.(Retd.) L.S. Shaw, RNR). (7)
24 Jul 1943 P.M., HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) ended her 5th war patrol (4th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (7)
8 Aug 1943 At 1430 B/8, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) departed Malta for her 6th war patrol (5th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off Brindisi.
She was escorted out until 1800 B/8 by HMS Sharpshooter (Lt.Cdr. W.L. O'Mara, DSC, RN).

HMS Unruly left Malta through the eastern swept channel through positions 35°05°N, 16°31'E at 1000 B/9, 37°08'N, 17°14'E at 0500 B/10, 39°44'N, 18°52'E at 0500/11, 40°40'N, 18°52'E at 0200 B/12, thence to patrol off Brindisi. Surface passage on the 10th. (24)
8 Aug 1943 For the daily and attack positions of HMS Unruly during her 6th war patrol see the map below.

11 Aug 1943 At 1618 A/11, in position 40°16'N, 18°53'E, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) commenced an attack on a large merchant vessel which was subsequently identified as the Italian hospital ship Gradisca (13868 GRT, built 1913).
At 1654 A/11, a dummy attack was carried out on this hospital ship from a range of 1000 yards. (7)

11 Aug 1943 At 0410 B/11, in position 39°47'N, 18°54'E, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) encountered HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN). The two submarines passed only at a range of 100 to 200 yards.
HMS Unruly reported the encounter at 0409 B/11 in position 39°48'N, 18°53'E.
Both submarines realised the identity of the other submarines. HMS Uproar challenged and HMS Unruly replied with a signal grenade. (25)
12 Aug 1943 At 0517 B/12, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) dived on the limit of her patrol area and closed the harbour entrance of Brindisi during the forenoon.
At 1350 B/12, a small southbound destroyer was seen close inshore.

At 1355 B/12, smoke was seen to the southward which was closed. The source of this smoke was later identified as a minesweeping trawler and two auxiliary schooners, all sweeping. Their track coincided with the known south-eastern approach route.
At 1500 B/12, the 'small destroyer' seen earlier was seen to enter harbour after hanging about for a while off the harbour entrance while probably waiting for the gate to be opened. (7)
13 Aug 1943 At 0510 B/13, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) dived and commenced to close the harbour entrance of Brindisi.
At 0825 B/13, two minesweepers were seen to leave the harbour.
At 1000 B/13, two motor launches left the harbour and searched the area to the southward.
At 1800 B/13, two corvettes, looking like Gabbiano-class but with the addition of a gun mounting aft, left harbour and went out through the south-east channel.
At 1829 B/13, a motor launch altered course towards at a range of 1500 yards. HMS Unruly went deep.

At 1832 B/13, a pattern of three depth charges was dropped uncomfortably close. HMS Unruly had obviously been detected so the submarine commenced to retire to seaward. Eight more depth charges were dropped subsequently.
At 1850 B/13, HE was picked up and shortly afterwards Asdic transmissions were heard.
Between 1850 B/13 and 1905 B/13, six depth charges were dropped but these were gradually getting further away.

At 1950 B/13, HMS Unruly returned to periscope depth. A corvette was seen quite a long way off on the starboard quarter making a lot of noise. Another corvette was seen on the port beam at a range of about 1000 yards. This vessel was steaming to seaward on a parallel course to HMS Unruly. Course was altered 20° to starboard to open slightly and then HMS Unruly went deep again.
At 2056 B/13, HMS Unruly returned to periscope depth. There was nothing in sight.
At 2230 B/13, HMS Unruly surfaced and withdrew 15 miles from the land to charge.
The following morning, HMS Unruly again closed the harbour entrance but nothing was seen all day. (7)
15 Aug 1943 HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) torpedoed and damaged the Italian tanker Cesco (6161 GRT, built 1917) in position 027°, Pedagne Light, 2.3 nautical miles (off Brindisi, Italy).

The Cesco was en-route from Brindisi to Valona and was escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Enrico Cosenz and the Italian corvettes Pomona and Chimera. Following the attack HMS Unruly was depth charged by Pomona.

Later the same day HMS Unruly attacked another convoy. This convoy was made up of the Italian transports Goggiam (1994 GRT, built 1922) and Città di Spezia (2474 GRT, built 1929) as well as the Italian tugs Nettuno and Asinara. They were escorted by the Italian naval auxiliary Lubiana. Four torpedoes were fired at both of the transport ships but no hits were obtained. Position of this attack was 40°52'N, 17°58'E. [Note: Goggiam was an engine-aft ship and thus looked like a tanker.]
0525 B/15 Dived and patrolled off Brindisi.

0705 B/15 - Sighted the funnel of a southbound destroyer at long range, The mast was not seen against the land, and the funnel was fist though to be the conning tower of a submarine. Ran in at full submerged speed for 10 minutes.
0717 B/15 - Returned to periscope depth. Identified the target as a destroyer. Meanwhile minesweepers were seen to leave the harbour and sweep along the coast to the south-eastward.
0915 B/15 - Sighted a northbound tug towing a floating crane. Considered attacking the tug but decided it was too small to be bothered about and to await bigger 'game'.

1025 B/15 - Sighted a southbound tanker escorted by two Gabbiano-class corvettes and a old type torpedo boat. A flying boat was overhead. The corvettes were stationed on either bow and the destroyer was on the port quarter. Started attack.
1036 B/15 - The tanker was seen to be of about 5000 tons and was in ballast.

1050 B/15 - Fired four torpedoes from 1300 yards. Enemy course was 114°, speed 8 knots. Two torpedo hits were heard. Another torpedo was heard exploding on hitting the land. Unruly had gone deep on firing.
1130 B/15 - Returned to periscope depth. Sighted the target being beached. A corvette was on our starboard quarter at a range of only 500 yards so immediately went deep again.
1132 B/15 - The corvette must have sighted us as she dropped a pattern of 13 depth charges fairly close.
1159 B/15 - Another pattern of 13 depth charges was dropped. This was a bit further off.
1220 B/15 - Another pattern of 13 depth charges was dropped. A long way off.
1240 B/15 - Returned to periscope depth. Continued to retire from the area.
1250 B/15 - Commenced reload.
1415 B/15 - Stopped reload and stopped all auxiliary machinery as a corvette came near.
1515 B/15 - Continued reload. A number of masts could be seen to the eastward. These are thought to be A/S vessels.
1544 B/15 - Identified the masts and funnels of one of these ships as a northbound tanker about 10 nautical miles offshore. Altered course to close and started attack.
1550 B/15 - Reload was completed.
1628 B/15 - Fired four torpedoes at two targets in this new convoy, a tanker and a liner. The convoy's course was 310°, speed 5 knots. Range on firing was 5200 yards. No hits were obtained.
1715 B/15 - Returned to periscope depth. The convoy was still in sight, not quite hull down. The escorts were making smoke and no details could thus be made out.
Between 1742 B/15 and 1900 B/15, ten more depth charges were dropped.
1721 B/15 - Set course to return to Malta as all torpedoes had been expended.
2029 B/15 - All clear around. Surfaced. (7)
16 Aug 1943 At 0235 B/16, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) sighted an auxiliary schooner at long range. Lt. Fyfe did not close as it was suspected this may well be a 'Q-ship / submarine trap'.

At 0425 B/16, a signal timed 0340 B/16 was passed. [We have been unable to find the text of this signal but obviously it contained information about the attacks, that all torpedoes had been expended and that HMS Unruly was returning to Malta.
At 0500 B/16, HMS Unruly dived for the day.

At 1315 B/16, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0525 B/16 was received which contained the following; Your 0340 B/16. Well done. Return route through position 38°35'N, 18° 20'E at 0500 B/17, 35°45'N, 16°38'E 1200 B/18 to make rendezvous with the minesweeper 5 miles east of the end of the eastern channel at 0800 B/19. Surface passage on the 17th. Bombing restrictions are being arranged. (7)

17 Aug 1943 At 1020 B/17, HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) sighted a submarine bearing 050° at a range of 8 miles. Course was altered towards and the other submarine was challenged and the correct reply was received. The other submarine was HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) which was en-route to Malta. (26)
19 Aug 1943 At 1200 B/19, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) ended her 6th war patrol (5th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She was escorted in by HMS Hebe (Lt.Cdr. G. Mowatt, RD, RNR) which had joined around 0800 B/19. (7)
31 Aug 1943 At 1500 B/31, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) departed Malta for her 7th war patrol (6th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off Durazzo.

HMS Unruly left via the Eastern Channel passing though positions 35°56'N, 16°31'E at 0800 B/1, 36°24'N, 16°47'E at 2000 B/1, 39°03'N, 18°26'E at 2000 B/2, thence through 39°45'N, 18°52'E to pass 40°30'N, 18°52'E at 2000 B/3 thence to patrol north of 41°00'N and east of 18°45'E. Surface passage on the 2nd.
4 Sep 1943 At 0300 B/4, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) entered her patrol area.

At 0537 B/4, HMS Unruly dived in position 41°13'N, 19°00'E and commenced to close the harbour of Durazzo. By 1330 B/4, the harbour was in sight and it was seen that it was crammed with ships. There was a two-funnelled liner of about 15000 tonds, two more liners of about 10000 tons and several transport vessels.
At 1600 B/4, two schooner minesweepers were seen to return to harbour after sweeping the inshore route to the southward of Durazzo.

At 1730 B/4, a southbound merchant vessel of about 1000 tons was seen. It was not attacked because Lt. Fyfe considered it too small to give away the submarines position in an area where it was thought that the enemy was not expecting submarine operations. The vessel was seen to enter harbour half an hour later. (7)
5 Sep 1943 HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) attacked a merchant vessel with four torpedoes off Durazzo. No hits were obtained.
This was most probably the Italian merchant vessel Marco (1487 GRT, 1901) which had sailed at 0600 B/5 from Durazzo for Gravosa.
0539 B/5 - Dived in position 41.16.5'N, 19.22'E. This position was carefully chosen as being the most effective position to intercept inbound and outbound shipping.
0612 B/5 - Sighted an outward bound merchant vessel. Started attack. Visibility was however very bad.
0624 B/5 - Estimated the size of the target at 1000 tons. Broke off the attack to wait for bigger 'game'.
0639 B/5 - Sighted a second ship leaving Durazzo. Started attack.
0652 B/5 - The target altered course to the northward. Indentified the target as a 3000 tons merchant vessel. Continued the attack.

0701 B/5 - In position 41°16'N, 19°24'E fired four torpedoes from 1050 yards. No hits were obtained and it is thought the torpedoes might have ran under. The target was seen to return to Durazzo. Withdrew to the northward.

1108 B/5 - Sighted a Generale-class torpedo boat searching the central approach route. Subsequently this torpedo boat searched to the northward about 7 miles off shore and then withdrew to seaward. This torpedo boat did not came out of Durazzo.

2014 B/5 - Surfaced in position 41°25'N, 19°19'E and closed the land along the northern approach route and then the central approach route along the track of the torpedo boat which had been sighted during the forenoon. (7)
8 Sep 1943 At 0935 B/8, near Durazzo, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) sighted a Gabbiano-class corvette to the southward.
At 1030 B/8, the corvette closed towards while transmitting on Asdic. HMS Unruly went deep and withdrew to the northward.
At 1100 B/8, HMS Unruly returned to periscope depth. The corvette was some way off now.
At 1310 B/8, a second corvette was sighted to the southward.
At 1341 B/8, both corvettes were seen to enter harbour.
At 2117 B/8, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0955 B/8 was received which contained the following orders; Leave patrol to pass positions as in my signal timed 1115 B/7 but 48 hours later.
This signal referred to the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1115 B/7. This signal had been send to HMS Unshaken (Lt. J. Whitton, RN).

This signal contained the following orders (We have added 48 hours to the times the submarine was to pass through the respective positions; Leave patrol to pass through the following positions; 40°15'N, 19°08'E at 1200 B/11, 39°41'N, 19°03'E at 2300 B/11, 37°10'N, 17°30'E 2000 B/12, 35°45'N, 16°38'E at 1200/13 and make rendezvous with the minesweeper at 0800 B/14, 5 miles east of position 'B' of Q.B.B. 147 (mined area). Surface passage on 12th.
At 2236 B/8, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1935 B/8 was received. For the text of this signal see the other 'event' for 8 September 1943. (28)

8 Sep 1943 In a signal timed 1932 B/8, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to the submarines of the 10th Submarine Flotilla which were at sea on patrol in enemy waters. This were ORP Sokol (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) J.K.S. Koziolkowski, DSC), HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN), HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) and HMS Unshaken (Lt. J. Whitton, RN); Cease hostilities against Italy. Submarines identified as German are to be sunk. Maintain all normal precautions for your own safety. Sokol return to Malta by reverse of outward route E.T.A. 0800 B/11. Unruly conform with my signal timed 0955 B/8, 24 hours early. Unrivalled is to return, by same route 24 hours after . Unshaken conform with my signal timed 1115 B/7. (29)

10 Sep 1943 At 2012 B/10, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) surfaced in position 41°16'N, 19°19'E, and set course to return to Malta. On leaving patrol all shipping sighted on 4 September was still in harbour. (7)

12 Sep 1943 At 0005 B/12, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) sighted the lights of a ship to the southward. Speed was increased to full to close. The ship was subsequently seen to be part of a convoy which was made up of a total of eight ships. They were southbound.

At 0100 B/12, HMS Unruly encountered HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) which was escorting eight Italian ships that had escaped from Bari to Malta. Unruly then joined Unrivalled in escorting these ships.
[For more info on this convoy see the page of HMS Unrivalled, commencing on 10 September 1943.] (7)

13 Sep 1943 At 0800 B/13, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) was detached by HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) to make rendezvous with HMS Bergamot (Lt. R.T. Horan, RNR) and lead her to the convoy.

At 0803 B/9, a JU 88 was seen flying low over the leading ship of the convoy. Went to gun action stations. The aircraft flew to the rear of the convoy and then out to starboard, out of range. The aircraft subsequently altered to a closing course.

At 0806 B/9, HMS Unruly fired one round of 3" High Explosive fused to 2000 yards. On the round exploding the aircraft altered course away immediately. HMS Unruly then proceeded on her way to make rendezvous with HMS Bergamot.
At 1655 B/9, rendezvous was made with HMS Bergamot.
At 1832 B/9, the convoy was sighted.
At 1940 B/9, HMS Unruly parted company with the convoy to proceed independently to Malta. (7)
14 Sep 1943 At 1030 B/14, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) ended her 7th war patrol (6th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She was escorted in by HMS BYMS 2028 (Skr. J.R. Clark, RNR) which had joined at 0705 B/14. (7)
27 Sep 1943 HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) and HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) conducted exercises off Malta. (30)
1 Oct 1943 Around 1800 C/1, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) departed Malta for her 8th war patrol (7th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in the Aegean.
She was escorted out by HMS Bude (T/A/Lt.Cdr. E.A.C. Phillips, RNR) which parted company around 2130 C/1.
HMS Unruly was routed through the following positions; 35°50'N, 15°17'E 2230 C/1, 35°47'N, 17°30'E at 2200 C/2, 35°38'N, 23°01'E at 0700 C/4, thence to patrol in the western part of the Aegean. (31)
1 Oct 1943 For the daily and attack positions of HMS Unruly during her 8th war patrol see the map below.

2 Oct 1943 In a signal timed 1151 C/2, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN); 1) From position 35°38'N, 23°01'E proceed dived at 200 feet through the Antikithera Channel on course 066° on day 4. 2) Patrol between the Serpho Channel and Milos. You, if necessary, have freedom of action west of 26°30'E and south of 37°25'N. Submarines are not to be attacked in the Aegean unless definitely proved hostile. 3) You will be recalled from patrol to pass through the Kaso Strait on the 13th. (32)

3 Oct 1943 At 2230 C/3, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) received the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1513 C/3 which contained the following; Disregard paragraph 2 of my signal timed 1151 C/2. After passing the Antikithera Channel proceed with utmost despatch to operate off the south coast of Kos against enemy shipping employed landing troops in Kamaro Bay. HMS Unsparing (Lt. A.D. Piper, DSC and 2 bars, RNR) is operating in this area until dusk/5. My signal timed 1453 C/3 to HMS Unsparing and HMS Trooper (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSO, DSC, RN) refers.

This signal (1453 C/3) contained the following; My signal timed 0917 C/3. 1) Enemy landing in Kamaro Bay and on north coast east of 27°08'E using 7 transports, 2 destroyers, 2 E-Boat landing craft and Caiques. 2) Endeavour to attack close to the beach withdrawing to Turkish territorial waters when necessary. 3) Allied surface and air forces are instructed not to attack submarines. 4) Delay my signal timed 1153 C/2 by 24 hours.
At 2257 C/3, the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1901 C/3 was received which contained information on minefields in the Kos area. [We have been unable to find the text of this signal.] (33)
4 Oct 1943 At 0827 C/4, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) dived in position 35°30'30"N, 23°12'20"E and proceeded through the AntiKithera Channel at 200 feet.
At 1536 C/4, HMS Unruly surfaced in position 35°49'N, 23°33'E and proceeded at 10 knots. After the moon set the zig-zagging was stopped and the speed was 11.5 knots toward the Kos area. (7)
5 Oct 1943 At 0639 C/5, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) dived south-east of Stampalia, in position 36°31'N, 26°37'E.

At 1030 C/5, HMS Unruly was in position 36°39'N, 26°40'E. Lt. Fyfe had chosen this position to the west of Kos to patrol during the daylight hours until HMS Unsparing (Lt. A.D. Piper, DSC and 2 bars, RNR) would be clear of the area around Kos by dusk today.

At 1508 C/5, the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1312 C/5, was received in which HMS Unruly was ordered to patrol between Stampalia and Amorgos with despatch as it was feared that the German invasion of Leros may be attempted tonight.

At 2003 C/5, HMS Unruly surfaced in position 36°42'N, 26°27'E and closed Amorgos. Patrol was maintained close inshore during the night. The Piraeus - Rhodes shipping route ran past Amorgos and it was considered that this billet would prove more fruitful than the Stampalia side of the channel.
Nothing was however sighted during the night and the following day. (34)

6 Oct 1943 At 2339 C/6, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) received the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1922 C/6 [not 100% sure about the Time Of Origin (T.O.O.) of this signal as is difficult to read in both versions of the patrol reports in our collection] which contained information on friendly forces operating to the west of Leros at dawn.
[We have been unable to find the text of this signal but it must have referred to the cruiser / destroyer force mentioned in the other 'event' for 6 October 1943.] (7)

6 Oct 1943 Around 1100 C/6, the light cruisers HMS Penelope (Capt. G.D. Belben, DSC, AM, RN), HMS Sirius (Capt. P.B.W. Brooking, DSO, RN, Senior Officer) and the destroyers HMS Faulknor (Capt. A.K. Scott-Moncrieff, DSO, RN), HMS Fury (Lt.Cdr. T.F. Taylor, RN) and HMS Eclipse (Lt.Cdr. E. Mack, DSO, DSC, RN) departed Alexandria to proceed to the Aegean to attack enemy forces proceeding to Leros.

Around 2315 C/6 [source log of HMS Sirius, the log of HMS Penelope gives 0100 C/7], HMS Eclipse was detached to return to Alexandria due to defective steering. She arrived back at Alexandria around 1415 C/7.

The force arrived off Leros around 0530 C/7 but found no sign of a German invasion. At 0630 C/7 they picked up an enemy sighting from the submarine HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN). At 0800 C/7, they intercepted the convoy sighted by HMS Unruly and completely destroyed it. The convoy had been made up of the transport Olympos (852 GRT, built 1904), the landing barges F 308, F 327, F 336, F 494 and F 532. They were escorted by UJ 2111. F 496 was no longer with them, she had been damaged earlier by HMS Unruly and was beached on Stampalia.
HMS Penelope, HMS Sirius, HMS Faulknor and HMS Fury then set course to return to Alexandria via the Scarpanto Strait. The force came under heavy German air attacks by Ju-87's and Ju-88's.
Around 1120 C/7, the force was joined by the escort destroyers HMS Rockwood (Lt. S.R. Le H. Lombard-Hobson, RN) and Miaoulis (?) which came from Casteloriso.

Around 1215 C/7, during an attack by Ju-87 dive bombers HMS Penelope was hit aft by a bomb but it did not explode. There were also many near missed. Two officers and twenty-two ratings were killed and twenty-nine were wounded. HMS Penelope was able to continue at 22 knots.
At 1705 C/8, HMS Rockwood and RHS Miaoulis parted company.
HMS Sirius and HMS Faulknor arrived at Alexandria around 0050 C/8 followed by HMS Penelope and HMS Fury around 0200 C/8. (35)
7 Oct 1943 HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) heavily damaged the German landing vessel F 496 with gunfire east of Amorgos. Several other ships were lightly damaged with gunfire from HMS Unruly.

Beside the above mentioned F 496, the convoy attacked was made up of the German transport Olympos (852 GRT, built 1904), the landing vessels F 308, F 327, F 336, F 494 and F 532. They were escorted by the auxiliary submarine chaser UJ 2111. Shortly afterwards this convoy was attacked and destroyed by the British light cruisers HMS Sirius (Capt. P.B.W. Brooking, DSO, RN) and HMS Penelope (Capt. G.D. Belben, DSC, AM, RN) and the British destroyers HMS Faulknor (Capt. A.K. Scott-Moncrieff, DSO, RN) and HMS Fury (Lt.Cdr. T.F. Taylor, RN).
0401 C/7 - In position 36°46'N, 25°58'E sighted a number of darkened ships to the southward. Closed and then altered to a parallel course and maintained position on the contacts port beam.
0456 C/7 - Turned in to attack.

0501 C/7 - Fired four torpedoes on the swing aimed at the four rear ships. Range was about 1000 yards. No hits were obtained. On firing transmitted a contact signal in the hope it might lead our surface forces to this convoy. The attack appears not to have been noticed by the enemy.
0506 C/7 - Set of in chase of the convoy.

0600 C/7 - Gun action stations. Position was now 36°49'N, 26°16'E. Opened fire on the rear Siebel Ferry. The second round hit and after two more hits, shifted target to the next Siebel Ferry which was hit twice. The first target was now on fire. The gun then malfunctioned. After it was cleared one round was fired at the larger merchant vessel. It hit. Fire was then shifted to a Siebel Ferry coming out of the smoke. Hit it at least twice. The merchant then also came out of the smoke and fire was re-opened on this ship. It was hit eight times. It was then seen to be stopped and being abandoned. Altered course to the east to set off in chase of the remainder of the convoy. During the chase they opened fire on Unruly and therefore the chase was abandoned at 0635 C/7.

A total of 49 rounds of H.E.D.A. ammunition had been fired. 9 hits are thought to have been obtained on the transport, 3 on a small transport or tug and at least 10 hits on 3 or 4 Siebel Ferries [The Siebel Ferries were actially F-boats].

0635 C/7 - Shells assumed to be from own surface forces were seen bursting around the convoy. Course was altered to the westward to close the first transport which could be seen stopped. The transport altered course away and it is thought she had been picking up survivors.
0710 C/7 - Dived in position 37°46'N, 26°19'E, for four aircraft which came towards in formation.
0720 C/7 - Three destroyers were seen to be engaging the convoy. One of these was identified as HMS Fury (Lt.Cdr. T.F. Taylor, RN).

0752 C/7 - From a range of 3000 yards HMS Unruly was able to watch the destroyers to blow up the transport with a torpedo. It was evident she must have been carrying ammunition. HMS Unruly then withdrew to the westward. Many survivors / bodies were seen in the water, some swimming, some floating face down and some in boats and rubber dinghy's.
0945 C/7 - Sighted three R-boats closing from the south-west. They commenced to hunt astern, steering westwards. The R-boats came closer and HMS Unruly went deep and silenced all machinery.
0947 C/7 - One depth charge was dropped followed by three more. All were not close and quite a way off. One R-boat passed up the starboard side and two up the port side.
1204 C/7 - No more HE was heard. Came to periscope depth. All clear.
The three R-boats sighted were R 34, R 194 and R 210 which had been near the area of the action and then went in to pick up the survivors.
1350 C/7 - Two float planes were seen sweeping to the westward.
2000 C/7 - Surfaced in position 36°39'N, 26°02'E and patrolled to the west of this position until moonset when a billet between Amargos and Anedro was selected. (36)

8 Oct 1943 HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) torpedoed and sank the German auxiliary minelayer Bulgaria (1188 GRT, built 1894) south off Amorgos Island, Greece in position 36°43'N, 25°47'E. The Bulgaria was loaded with supplies and soldiers for the island of Kos and was proceeding together with the minelayer Drache.
0628 C/8 - Dived for the day, south of Amorgos, in position 36°41'N, 25°53'E.
1459 C/8 - Received a report from aircraft, Time Of Origin (T.O.O.) 1102 C/8, that an enemy convoy, made up of a transport and one escort was coming towards.
1639 C/8 - Sighted a ship coming towards. Shortly afterwards sighted a second ship. Two aircraft were seen patrolling the area. Started attack.

1706 C/8 - In position 36°43'N, 25°47'E fired four torpedoes from 1350 yards. One hit was obtained. HE of the target ceased immediately. On firing Unruly had gone to 100 feet, changed course and silenced all machinery.
1715 C/8 - Three depth charges were dropped. Close, but they caused no damage to Unruly. Four more depth charges were dropped 15 to 20 minutes later but these were further off.
1755 C/8 - Returned to periscope depth. The escort vessel only was in sight steering eastwards at a range of 8000 yards. A few minutes later she was lost from sight in the low visibility.

2000 C/8 - Surfaced in position 36°40'N, 25°38'E and withdrew from the area by passing between Santorini and Anaphi, thence set course for Kaso Strait and passage to Beirut as all torpedoes had been fired. (7)

9 Oct 1943 In a signal timed 0341 C/9, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) informed the Capt. S.1 that all torpedoes had been fired and that she was proceeding to Beirut. [We have been unable to find the text of this signal.] (7)
12 Oct 1943 At 0800 C/12, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) ended her 8th war patrol (7th in the Mediterranean) at Beirut where she joined the 1st Submarine Flotilla. (7)
18 Oct 1943 HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) conducted noise trials off Beirut but these were broken off / cancelled before they were completed. (37)
23 Oct 1943 At 1600 C/23, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) departed Beirut for her 9th war patrol (8th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in the Aegean in the Amorgos (general) area.

24 Oct 1943 At 1135 C/24, in approximate position 34°12'N, 31°57'E, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) sighted a submarine bearing 190°. This turned out to be HMS Severn (Lt.Cdr. A.N.G. Campbell, RN). (7)
26 Oct 1943 At 0520 C/26, in approximate position 36°06'N, 26°15'E, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN sighted another submarine). This turned out to be HMS Torbay (Lt. R.J. Clutterbuck, RN). (7)

26 Oct 1943 At 1926 C/26, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) surfaced in position 36°40'N, 26°01'E and set course to pass between Amorgos and Kinaros. While still to the southward of these two islands occasional radar transmissions were picked up to the eastward. The bearing of these transmissions altered from 080° to 010° during the course of two hours, but no echo was obtained.

At 2350 C/26, after clearing Kinaros a group of four echoes were picked up bearing 010° at a range of 7000 yards. Course was altered towards at full surface speed. The bearing altered to the southwards and the range increased slowly. The chase was abandoned after 15 minutes when the range was 10000 yards. The targets speed was estimated as 20 knots and it is considered unlikely that four enemy ships capable of this speed would be encountered and they are thus thought to have been friendly as own surface forces were known to be in the area.

This was indeed correct as the destroyer HMS Faulknor (Capt. M.S. Thomas, DSO, RN) and the escort destroyers HMS Belvoir (Lt. J.F.D. Bush, DSC and Bar, RN), HMS Beaufort (Lt. J.R.L. Moore, RN), HMS Hursley (Lt.Cdr. W.J.P. Church, DSC and Bar, RN) and HMS Aldenham (A/Lt.Cdr. J.I. Jones, DSO, DSC, RNR) were on an anti shipping sweep in this area during the night of 26/27 October. (38)
31 Oct 1943 HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) attacked the German auxiliary patrol vessel GA 44 with gunfire north of Amorgos. As Unruly's gun malfunctioned the action had to be broken off.
The Germans claimed some hits on the submarine but this was not the case. Three Arado floatplanes were sent to patrol the area.
1140 C/31 - Sighted a schooner of approximately 100 tons bearing 074° and coming towards.

1226 C/31 - Surfaced in position 37°00N, 25°53'E for gun action. Opened fire at a range of 3800 yards. The gun malfuntioned after two rounds. The enemy (flying the German ensign) meanwhile had opened fire so the action had to be broken off.
1235 C/31 - Dived to periscope depth. The enemy was seen to come towards. Went deep and silenced all machinery.
1246 C/31 - The first of 8 depth charges was dropped but a long way off. The enemy carried out a hydrohone hunt as she was not fitted with an Asdic.
1305 C/31 - Returned to periscope depth.
1330 C/31 - The enemy altered course to the west.
1440 C/31 - The floatplanes arrived and patrolled the area for a while. (39)

3 Nov 1943 At 0651 C/3, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) dived in position 36°59'N, 25°36'E, and closed the eastern end of Amorgos to the 100 fathom line, half a mile from the shore. Course was then altered to the westward and a reconnaissance of the north coast of Amorgos was carried out in accordance with the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1811 B/2 [unable to find the text of this signal]. Nothing of interest was sighted and course was set to return to the patrol area to the south of Denusa. (7)

4 Nov 1943 In a signal timed 1456 B/4, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, DSC, RN) and HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN); 1) Unruly moving to patrol between Amorgos and Anaphi. Unrivalled moving to patrol in the west Aegean at the Commanding Officers discretion except that disturbance required in Milos possibly 5th/6th. 2) Unrivalled leave patrol diving to 200 feet through Antikithera Channel on 8th on course 246° from position 35°52'N, 23°41'E for 36 miles. 3) Capt. S.10 is requested arrange onward routeing to Malta.

In a signal timed 2206 B/4, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to HMS Unrivalled and HMS Unruly; 1) Cancel paragraph 1 of my signal timed 1456 B/4 and resume previous patrols. Reports indicate enemy movements from westward shortly. 2) Unruly cancel my signal timed 1811 B/2 as destroyers will shoot up Amorgos during the night of 5/6 November. (40)

5 Nov 1943 At 2306 C/5, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) received the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1703 B/5 which contained the following orders; Adjust patrol so as to pass position 'A' = 35°06'N, 26°44'E at 2300 B/7 thence proceeding on surface through position 'B' = 34°25'N, 29°59'E to arrive at Beirut at 0615 B/10.

At 2340 C/5, in position 37°01'N, 25°57'E, two British destroyers were sighted. It is thought this were probably HMS Faulknor (Capt. M.S. Thomas, DSO, RN), HMS Echo (Lt.Cdr. R.H.C. Wyld, RN) proceeding southwards to leave the Aegean. (41)

6 Nov 1943 In a signal timed 1853 B/6, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to the submarines mentioned; 1) HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) patrol between Anedro and Amorgos delaying my signal timed 1703 B/5 by 24 hours. 2) ORP Sokol (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) J.K.S. Koziolkowski, DSC) patrol between Anedro and Anaphi until 2359 B/7th then between Amorgos and Anaphi. (40)

7 Nov 1943 At 0032 C/7, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) received an enemy report on a convoy made up of two small merchant vessels, twelve landing vessels / barges and two escorts. This convoy had been in position 37°08'N, 25°20'E at 2340 hours on the 6th.

At 0034 C/7, an enemy report on a convoy made up of two small merchant vessels, six landing vessels / barges was received. This convoy had been in position 37°12'N, 25°20'E at 0005 hours. Both these convoy were reported as proceeding southwards at 8 knots.
At 0130 C/7, HMS Unruly arrived in position 36°47'N, 25°41'E to intercept. It was thought possible the convoys would be passing the vicinity of the patrol position around 0330 C/7.
At 0231 C/7, two flares were seen bearing 280° at a range of 10 miles. Also gunfire was heard. Closed with the Asdic watch closed up as it was a very dark night.
At 0300 C/7, the HE of a motor torpedo boat was reported right ahead.

At 0305 C,7, a bow wave was seen at a range of about 3000 yards. Unruly dived. The HE passed close down the starboard side. It is not sure the HE was that of a motor torpedo boat or a number of destroyers at high speed but due to the size of the bow wave this was considered more likely. It is thus very well possible this were friendly destroyers withdrawing after their engagement with the convoys.
At 0308 C/7, HMS Unruly surfaced in position 36°46'N, 25°39'E after the HE had faded astern.

[The destroyers HMS Penn (Lt.Cdr. J.H. Swain, DSO, RN) and HMS Pathfinder (Lt.Cdr. C.W. Malins, DSC and Bar, RN) had conducted an anti-shipping sweep in the Paros / Naxos area during the night and shortly after midnight they had sunk an enemy caique. This was the German patrol vessel GA 45.] (7)
8 Nov 1943 Between 2330 C/7 and 0030 C/8, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) sighted starshell to the north of Amorgos. Lt. Fyfe proceeded northwards to investigate.

At 0048 C/8, two darkened ships were seen steering to the southwest at a range of 2000 yards which were subsequently identified as two P-class destroyers. The recognition signal was flashed twice at the leading ship with a red shaded Aldis lamp. The leading ship switched on it's searchlight and the second ship followed suit. HMS Unruly was illuminated for over a minute by the searchlights during which time the identification signal was flashed several times. Eventually the searchlights were extinguished.
This must have been HMS Penn (Lt.Cdr. J.H. Swain, DSO, RN) and HMS Pathfinder (Lt.Cdr. C.W. Malins, DSC and Bar, RN) again. (7)
11 Nov 1943 At 0745 C/11, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) ended her 9th war patrol (8th in the Mediterranean) at Beirut. (7)
24 Nov 1943 HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) conducted noise trials off Beirut. (42)

28 Nov 1943 At 1730 B/28, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) departed Beirut for her 10th war patrol (9th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in the northern Aegean after patrolling along the west coast of Scarpanto and Kandelioussa.
