1 Dec 1943 At 0001 B/1, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN), after having passed the Kaso Strait, patrolled in the vicinity of position 35°22'N, 26°39'E as the patrol orders stated that the northern and western coasts of Kaso and Scarpanto were to be examined in daylight.
At 0610 B/1, HMS Unruly dived in position 35°22'N, 26°44'E and proceeded up the coast of Kaso and Scarpanto. No shipping was sighted though.
At 1812 B/1, HMS Unruly surfaced in position 35°40'N, 27°06'E and then set course to pass close westward of Kadelusia thence to pass through the Kinaros Channel. (7)
2 Dec 1943 HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) sank a Greek sailing vessel with gunfire south of Levitha island, Greece.
According to German records this vessel was named Melenova. Of the crew, three German soldiers and three Greeks survived. One German was killed.
0617 B/2 - Sighted a 2-masted caique of about 50 tons 5000 yards to the north-east. As dawn was breaking, dived and closed.

0645 B/2 - Surfaced for gun action on the enemy's port quarter and opened fire at a range of 4000 yards. Thirty rounds were fired for only four hits due to the heavy weather. The crew of two abandoned ship in a small rowing boat. The range was then closed and twenty-eight more rounds were expanded.
0710 B/2 - The target sank in position 36°53'N, 26°29'E heaving received a further eight hits.

1224 B/2 - When in position 36°58'N, 26°12.5'E, received an enemy report from an aircraft of one F-boat and three caiques anchored in position 36°55'N, 25°56'E (off north-east Amorgos). It was not possible to reach this position before dark. Lt. Fyfe decided to patrol in position 37°02.5'N, 26°15'E until 0100 B/3 in case the enemy was eastbound towards, for instance Leros.
The German F-boat F 124 was at Amorgos in a damaged state and may thus well have been the F-boat reported by the aircraft. (44)

4 Dec 1943 At 1335 B/4, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) received the Capt. S.1's signal timed 0937 B/4 in which it was stated that two enemy merchant vessels were due to leave Piraeus for the Dodecanese or Crete. These might well be encountered tonight.
In a signal timed 0125 B/5, the Capt. S.1 reported that no shipping had yet left Piraeus.
[The first merchant vessel to leave Piraeus only left in the evening of the 6th though.] (45)
5 Dec 1943 At 1235 B/5, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) received an enemy report of a merchant vessel of about 1000 tons and a caique in Syros harbour and of two caiques in Panormos harbour.

At 1330 B/5, in position 37°36'N, 25°22'E, sighted a two-masted caique bearing 320° at a range of 1000 yards proceeding on course 050°. Did not attack the vessel as Lt. Fyfe considered there was a strong possibility that the merchant vessel reported at Syros would be coming through the Mykoni Channel.
At 1525 B/5, the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1241 B/5 was received in which it was reported that caiques had been reported in Panormos harbour. (7)
6 Dec 1943 HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) destroyed the Greek caiques SYR 335 / Evangelistria and SYR 636 / Vassiliki with gunfire at Panormos.
0415 B/6 - Set course to dive off Panormos harbour (Tinos Island).
0626 B/6 - Dived in position 37°42'N, 25°22'E.
0800 hours - Sighted masts of two caiques in Panormos harbour and commenced closing.

1010 hours - From position 37°39'N, 25°04'E fired two torpedoes set to 2 feet, one aimed at each caique from a range of 800 yards. Both torpedoes missed and exploded on hitting the land. Retired from the area. As no guns could be seen on shore decided to return for gun action shortly before dawn.
1430 hours - Commenced closing the harbour again.
1729 hours - Gun action stations. Surfaced in position 37°39'N, 25°05'E and opened fire on the larger caique from a range of 2000 yards.
1750 hours - After expending all ammunition withdrew from the area. The target was not seen to sink but must have bean at least badly damaged. (7)
7 Dec 1943 HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) attacked the German transport Leda (4572 GRT, built 1943, former Italian Leopardi) with four torpedoes. No hits were obtained.

The Leda was escorted by the German destroyer TA 15 (former Italian Francesco Crispi), the German torpedo boat TA 16 (former Italian Castelfidardo) and the German motor minesweeper R 211. The Germans reported two water-columns at 2208 A/7 [note the one hour time zone difference.] and suspected an enemy submarine had made an attack. The convoy had been on passage from Leros to Samos.

[Note; on 20 November 1943 the Germans renumbered the captured Italian destroyers torpedo boats; The destroyer Turbine had been named TA 14 by the Germans and this remained so. The destroyer Francesco Crispi had originally been numbered TA 17 by the Germans was renumbered TA 15. The torpedo boat Calatafimi had originally been numbered TA 15 by the Germans was renumbered TA 19. The torpedo boat Castelfidardo had originally been numbered TA 16 by the Germans and remained TA 16. The torpedo boat San Martino had originally been numbered TA 18 by the Germans was renumbered TA 17. The torpedo boat Solferino had originally been numbered TA 19 by the Germans was renumbered TA 18.]
0125 B/7 - Received the Capt. S.1's signal timed 0035 B/7 which ordered HMS Unruly to patrol off Port Vathi, Samos forthwith.
0147 B/7 - When in position 37°39'N, 25°28'E, and having decoded the signal, course was set for Port Vathi at 11 knots.
0633 B/7 - Dived in position 37°51'N, 26°27'E.
0907 B/7 - In position 37°51.5'N, 26°37.5'E sighted an F-boat steering 120°. The range could not be closed to less than 6000 yards so fire was witheld.
[We have so dar been unable to identify this 'F-boat'.]
1500 B/7 - Arrived near Port Vathi. Sighted two motor torpedo boats leaving Port Vathi to the eastward.
This must have been the German S 36 and S 55 which had called at Port Vathi during their passage from Piraeus to Leros and departed around this time.
1809 B/7 - Surfaced and patrolled about 4 miles to the north of Port Vathi.
2240 B/7 - Sighted a darkened ship to the west and closed to investigate. Shortly afterwards identified the contact as a large ship and two smaller ships in line ahead.
2247 B/7 - Dived to complete the attack.
2302 B/7 - In position 37°52'N, 26°57'E fired four torpedoes at a 2000 tons merchant vessel escorted by two destroyers. No hits were obtained.
2316 B/7 - A depth charge was dropped.
2318 B/7 - Two depth charges were dropped but they were a long way off. Two destroyers were seen searching on the starboard quarter.
2335 B/7 - Altered course to the north-east.
0017 B/8 - Surfaced.
0029 B/8 - Sighted a motor torpedo boat closing at speed from the starboard beam. Dived to evade and altered course to 340°.
Between 0100 B/8 and 0140 B/8 - On three occasions a diesel engine was heard to start up, run for a minute or two and then stop.
0140 B/8 - No.3 and No.4 tubes had been reloaded. Surfaced and closed the harbour entrance. Nothing was sighted and course was altered to the westward. No further HE was heard.
Most likely the 'motor torpedo boat' encountered was the motor minesweeper R 211. (46)
8 Dec 1943 At 0514 B/8, north of Samos, in position 37°54'N, 26°52'E, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) picked up destroyer HE bearing 150°. Closed to investigate.
At 0520 B/8, the destroyer stopped. Lt. Fyfe considered that this destroyer was hunting the submarine and had stopped to listen for HE. [The 'destroyer' heard was most likely either TA 15 or TA 16.]
At 0532 B/8, HMS Unruly dived to close the destroyer with all machinery silenced to try to get a shot at the destroyer at dawn. Intermittent Asdic transmissions were heard bearing 160°.
At 0555 B/8, destroyer HE was picked up bearing 150° doing 150 revolutions. The HE was crossing the bow.

At 0610 B/8, HE of a reciprocating engine doing 70 revolutions thought to be from last night's target and a diesel engine doing 150 revolutions was heard on the same bearing as the destroyers. [This diesel engine was most likely R 211.]
At 0615 B/8, dawn was breaking. A ship was sighted Red 20° but it could not be identfied.
At 0622 B/8, the HE of the merchant vessel was lost.
At 0628 B/8, a destroyer passed ahead.
At 0650 B/8, a ship could be seen against the western horizon. It was a merchant vessel at a range of about 7000 yards. No escort could be seen.
At 0702 B/8, all HE was lost. Commenced to close Port Vathi.

The German convoy that had been attacked the previous evening [see the 'event' for 7 December] had departed Port Vathi around 0500 A/8 for Piraeus where they arrived around 1730 A/8. [note the one hour time zone difference.] On board the Leda were over 5400 Italian PoWs. If the ship had been sunk it would have meant that a huge loss of life was to be expected.
By 1200 B/8, it was seen that there was no shipping in Port Vathi.

At 1245 B/8, in positiom 37°47.5'N, 26°57'E, a merchant vessel was sighted which was closed at speed but subsequently was seen to be the Turkish Krom (3359 GRT, built 1927) which was heading for Samos Strait. The vessel was well outside Turkish territorial waters and might thus be attacked. However, as the B.B.C. had reported that the Turkish President was in a conference with the U.S. President Roosevelt and British Prime Minister Churchill, Lt. Fyfe considered it 'not wise' to fire at this ship. A dummy attack was carried out instead.

At 1827 B/8, the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1525 B/8 was received which contained the following orders; Shift patrol at dusk/8 to cover the north eastern approaches to the Steno Pass and Mykoni Channel.
After decoding the signal course was set for the Steno Pass.

At 2230 B/8, north-west of Samos, in position 37°53'N, 26°26'E sighted a small dark object. This was though may be a motor torpedo boat so Unruly turned stern on. No HE was however picked up and course was now reversed to close.

At 2300 B/8, the vessel was identified as a small boat with nets out with a crew of three men. Decided not to molest them so set course to the north. When the vessel was out of sight course was resumed for the Steno Pass. (47)

10 Dec 1943 In a signal timed 1751 B/10, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN); Leave present patrol area at 0001 B/11 and proceed patrolling off Port Vathi, Samos. (48)

11 Dec 1943 In a signal timed 1649 B/11, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN); You are to leave patrol P.M./12 thence by standard route (see my signal timed 1423 C/7(Nov), to arrive in position 35°06'N, 26°44'E at 0400 B/14 thence through position 34°25'N, 29°59'E at 0800 B/15 to arrive at Beirut 1600 B/16. (48)

12 Dec 1943 In a signal timed 0928 B/12, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN); Remain on patrol until P.M./13 then act in accordance with my signal timed 1649 B/11. All times 24 hours late. (48)

12 Dec 1943 At 1140 B/12, north of Samos, in position 37°49'N, 26°51'E, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) sighted the Swedish Red Cross relief ships Bardaland (2956 GRT, built 1936) and Camelia (1688 GRT, built 1941). (7)
13 Dec 1943 HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) fires two torpedoes at the German auxiliary minelayer Drache off Samos, Greece. Both torpedoes missed their target.

The convoy attacked by HMS Unruly was made up of the above mentioned Drache (which was thought to be a merchant vessel). She was escorted by the German destroyers TA 15, TA 14 the German motor torpedo boats S 36, S 55 and the German motor minesweeper R 211. They were en-route from Piraeus to Port Vathi (Samos).
The convoy had departed Piraeus around 1430 A/12 and arrived at Port Vathi around 0630 A/13. At 0440 A/13, a detonation was heard and a column of water was seen about 300 meters behind TA 15.
After having unloaded troops and their equipment the convoy departed Port Vathi around 1545 A/13 and arrived back at Piraeus around 0620 A/13. On the return trip PoWs were transported.

0505 B/13 - Sighted two darkened ships to the north-west. Altered course towards. Shortly afterwards other ships were sighted and the whole was identified as a convoy of one merchant vessel escorted by two destroyers (stationed on either quarter) and two E-boats (stationed on either bow).
0518 B/13 - Dived to complete the attack.
0534 B/13 - In position 37°52'N, 26°54'E fired two torpedoes at the merchant vessel from about 1000 yards. No hits were obtained.
0542 B/13 - An E-boat passed overhead but did not drop any depth charges.
0700 B/13 - Commenced closing Port Vathi.
0835 B/13 - Sighted the target hull down in the harbour. Also one destroyer and two E-boats were seen proceeding up and down off the harbour entrance.
0848 B/13 - Left the area to return to Beirut as all torpedoes had been expended.
1428 B/13 - Received part of the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1038 B/13. The signal was corrupt.

The text of this signal was to be the following; My 1649 B/11. Cancel paragraph 2. Leave patrol P.M./13 passing close westward Nikaria and Kinaros to eastward of Stampalia to arrive in position 35°38'N, 27°45'E at 0600 B/15th. Thence diving by day to arrive in position 35°26'N, 28°19'E at 1800 B/15th. Thence on the surface to position 34°17'N, 31°29'E at 1500 B/16 to arrive at Beirut at 1500 B/17. Bombing restrictions have been arranged.

1448 B/13 - Surfaced to pass Unruly's signal timed 1436 B/13 which contained the following; All torpedoes expended, regret no hits. Complying with your 0928 B/12. Merchant ship, destroyer and two E-Boats in Port Vathi at 0900 B/13.
On surfacing a large caique was seen bearing 180° at a range of 4 miles. The caique soon altered course away.

1750 B/13 - The Capt. S.1's signal timed 1038 B/13 was received in full. As Lt. Fyfe had just send a signal confirming to the previous orders he considered that he was bound to carry out his intentions unless a further signal was received from the Capt. S.1.
2345 B/13 - In position 37°32.5'N, 25°54'E, a submarine was sighted bearing 110° at a range of 4000 yards. This was HMS Sportsman (Lt. R. Gatehouse, DSC, RN). (49)
15 Dec 1943 In a signal timed 2150 B/15, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) informed the Capt. S.1 that she would arrive at Beirut at 0900 B/17. (7)
17 Dec 1943 At 0900 B/17, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) ended her 10th war patrol (9th in the Mediterranean) at Beirut. (7)
19 Dec 1943 At 1700 B/19, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) departed Beirut for Port Said. (50)
20 Dec 1943 Around 1130 B/20, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) arrived at Port Said where she is immediately docked. (50)
23 Dec 1943 Around 1230 B/23, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) is undocked at Port Said and immediately departed for Beirut. (50)
24 Dec 1943 Around 1530 B/24, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) arrived at Beirut from Port Said. (50)
3 Jan 1944 At 1700 B/3, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) departed Beirut for her 11th war patrol (10th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in the Aegean.
At 2012 B/3, HMS Unruly encountered HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) which arrived at Beirut the following morning.

4 Jan 1944 At 1830 B/4, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) and HMS Torbay (Lt. R.J. Clutterbuck, RN) encountered each other. After exchanging recognition signals both continued on their way, HMS Unruly to the Aegean on patrol and HMS Torbay to Beirut. (51)
6 Jan 1944 At 0100 B/6, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) commenced the northbound passage of the Kaso Strait on the surface.
At 0654 B/6, HMS Unruly dived in position 36°07'N, 26°19'E and continued her passege northwards.
In a signal timed 1829 B/6, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to HMS Unruly; Proceed to patrol south of Lemnos to the westward of longitude 25°36'E. (7)
7 Jan 1944 At 0802 B/7, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN), which had remained on the surface longer making use of the bad visibility as she was a bit behind schedule, dived in position 37°58'N, 25°16'E.

At 1425 B/7, when in position 38°22'N, 25°07'E, the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1149 B/7 was received which contained orders to proceed with all despatch to the north-east approaches to the Doro Channel.
At 1752 B/7, HMS Unruly surfaced in position 38°21'N, 24°59'E.
At 1930 B/7, HMS Unruly arrived in position 090° - Cape Doro - 6 miles.

At 2114 B/7, a merchant vessel escorted by two destroyers was sighted bearing 015° at a range of 6000 yards. Unruly was right ahead of the merchant vessel. Her escorts were stationed on either bow. The moon was practically overhead but was slightly clouded over. In view of this and the very rough sea Lt. Fyfe considered that it might be possible to penetrate the screen and attack on the surface. There was however insufficient time to break through the screen and Lt. Fyfe decided to attack from the port bow which had a bit the advantage of the submarine being silhouetted against the land from the port wing destroyer. The attack was commenced.

Enemy course was estimated as being 200° at 9 knots. The target looked similar to the Bacchus (Former French wine tanker, 1810 GRT, built 1922) [This ship was renamed Bertha by the Germans later] which had been reported to have left the Dardanelles on the night of the 6th.
At 2128 B/7, the port wing destroyer was seen to turn towards when the range was 1500 yards.
At 2129 B/7, HMS Unruly dived to 80 feet and silenced all machinery.
At 2130 B/7, one depth charge was dropped. This caused some minor damage to electric light bulbs but fortunately no more were dropped.
At 2133 B/7, course was set to withdraw to the northward. One destroyer remained in the area to hunt using Asdic.
At 2145 B/7, three faint explosions were heard which are believed to have been acoustic charges.
At 2202 B/7, HMS Unruly surfaced. There was nothing in sight and the submarine withdrew 5 miles to seaward.

The convoy was indeed made up of the Bacchus which was en-route from the Dardanelles (departed around 0800 A/7) to Piraeus (arrived around 0500 A/8) escorted by the German torpedo boat TA 16 (former Italian Castelfidardo) and the auxiliary submarine chaser UJ 2110 (former Greek minelayer Korgialenios). It was TA 16 which detected the submarine and attacked with depth charges and later intimidation charges. The A/S hunt was difficult due to the heavy sea conditions. (52)
8 Jan 1944 At 1954 B/8, in position 38°09'N, 24°43'E, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) sighted a darkened ship bearing 330° at a range of 6000 yards. The submarine commenced to close.
At 2020 B/8, the target was lost from sight against the land. When last seen, at a range of 3000 yards the silhouette was similar to that of a destroyer but Lt. Fyfe is not sure that it was one.
At 2023 B/8, the enemy vessel fired a starshell. HMS Unruly dived. On diving HE was picked up bearing 265°. It may have been a destroyer. Revolutions were subsequently counted at 170.
At 2130 B/8, the HE faded.
At 2149 B/8, HMS Unruly surfaced. There was nothing in sight.

The vessel encountered was the German auxiliary submarine chaser UJ 2105 which was on passage from Skyros to Piraeus. The originally had to be part of the escort of the convoy that was attacked the previous evening but she was unable to do so due to the bad weather conditions. This was also the reason why she continued her passage to Piraeus instead of hunting the submarine which they thought they had sighted. (53)
10 Jan 1944 HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) sank the Greek caique PI 116 / Agios Nikolaos (102 GRT) with gunfire off Cape Doro, Andros Island, Greece.
Later the same day the Greek caique PI 299 / Themistokles (44 GRT) was sunk with gunfire off Cape Gria, Andros, Greece.
1429 B/10 - Sighted a large caique (about 100 tons) bearing 270°. Course 090°. Started to close.
1641 B/10 - Surfaced in position 38°12'N, 24°38'E. Opened fire wth the gun from 4000 yards. Fired ten rounds from this range for two hits. Then closed the range to 1500 yards.
1704 B/10 - By now thirty-six hits had been fired and many hits had been obtained. The target was on fire and in a sinking condition. Dived.
1717 B/10 - The fire did not seem to have a very strong hold so surfaced and re-engaged the target. Fired ten more rounds, all hit.
1723 B/10 - The target was now burning fiercely. Dived.
2100 B/10- Sighted sails of a southbound caique against the land at a range of 10000 yards. Closed.
2209 B/10 - Engaged a small (estimated 40 tons) caique with gunfire in position 37°55'N, 24°57'E from a range of 1000 yards. Ten Rounds were fired, all hit. The target was seen to sink. (7)
11 Jan 1944 HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) sank thee Greek caiques in Panormos harbour, Tinos, Greece. The last two were sunk in position 37°39'N, 25°05'E.
0200 B/11 - Arrived in the eastern approaches to the Steno Pass.
0655 B/11 - Dived in position 37°44'N, 25°06'E.
0825 B/11 - Sighted a large caique entering Panormos harbour.
1430 B/11 - Commenced closing Panormos harbour.
1610 B/11 - Sighted three caiques inside Panormos harbour. Their seizes were estimated as being 150, 100 and 40 tons. The largest one was heavily laden.
1623 B/11 - Surfaced in position 37°39'N, 25°05'E at a range of 1000 yards from the caique. All three were then sunk with gunfire. A total of thirty-three rounds were fired.
1632 B/11 - Withdrew from the harbour and dived.
1726 B/11 - Received the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1510 B/11 which contained information that an enemy tanker was due to leave Piraeus for the Dardanelles today. (7)
15 Jan 1944 HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) sank the Greek sailing vessel PI 997 / Aghios Giorgios (25 GRT) with gunfire north of Syros Island / west of the Steno Pass in position 37°38'N, 24°54'E.
0233 B/15 - In position 37°38'N, 24°54'E sighted a southbound caique bearing 230°, range 11000 yards. Closed at full speed.
0305 B/15 - Fired five rounds. The target, estimated to be of about 20 tons, was hit three times before it sank. (7)

15 Jan 1944 In a signal 1241 B/15, the listed submarines received the following orders from the Capt. S.1; 1) HMS Unsparing (Lt. A.D. Piper, DSC and 2 bars, RNR) patrol between Tenedos and lat. 39°38'N. HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) patrol between lat. 39°38'N and Cape Baba, Turkey. 2) Both patrols to be close to Turkish territorial waters and submarines to be in position by noon 16th. 3) Cancel my signal timed 0928 B/15. [We have been unable to find the text of this quoted signal.] (54)

16 Jan 1944 At 1305 B/16, in approximate position 39°26'N, 25°55'E, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) sighted a northbound merchant vessel inside Turkish territorial waters. The ship could not be identified but was most likely Turkish.
At 1627 B/16, in position 39°25'N, 26°03'E, a southbound merchant vessel was sighted. Started attack.
At 1630 B/16, the submarine broke surface on the targets beam. The target was seen to be Turkish and the attack was broken off.

At 1924 B/16, the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1731 B/16 was received which contained the following information; The German tanker [???, PLM 16 was a cargo vessel but perhaps she was converted to a tanker. Note that the PLM 16 was an engine-aft ship and looked thus like a tanker.] Peter (former French PLM 16, 3754 GRT, built 1921) arrived at Istanbul fully laden and in transit for the Aegean.
in a follow up signal timed 2117 B/16 a discription of the Peter was given and also the information that she was due to sail for the Aegean A.M./17. (55)

At 1924 B/16, the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1731 B/16 was received which contained the following information; The German tanker [???, PLM 16 was a cargo vessel but perhaps she was converted to a tanker] Peter (former French PLM 16, 3754 GRT, built 1921) arrived at Istanbul fully laden and in transit for the Aegean. (56)

18 Jan 1944 At 0050 B/18, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) received the Capt. S.1's signal timed 2204 B/17 which stated that the German transport (tanker ?) Peter (former French PLM 16, 3754 GRT, built 1921) was probably still at Istanbul awaiting an improvement in weather.
At 2217 B/18, the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1840 B/18 which stated that at 1030 B/18 the Peter was still at Istanboel at 1030 B/18. (55)

19 Jan 1944 At 0235 B/19, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) sighted the lights of a southbound merchant vessel. The range was closed to 2000 yards, keeping end on, to identify. The bright lights on deck however made it impossible to make out any details. HMS Unruly then followed the vessel up the Mesulim Channel.

At 0347 B/19, from a range of 500 yards it was seen that the target's funnel was amidships which did not fit the desciption of the German transport Peter so took no further action and set course to return to the vicinity of Cape Baba.

At 1735 B/19, the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1152 B/19 was received which contained the following orders for HMS Unruly; Leave patrol P.M./19 to arrive at Beirut 0645 B/24. [The route was included in the signal but we have been unable to find the entire text of this signal.]
At 1822 B/19, HMS Unruly surfaced in position 39°30'N, 25°59'E and set course to return to Beirut. (56)

21 Jan 1944 At 1623 B/21, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN), surfaced and bombarded an enemy traffic control station on Kandelioussa Island using the remaining ammunition for the 3" gun. Four hits were obtained but it appeared not much damage was done but it was hoped that a few Germans had a nasty 20 minutes. (7)
24 Jan 1944 At 0730 B/24, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) ended her 11th war patrol (10th in the Mediterranean) at Beirut. (7)
9 Feb 1944 At 0730 B/9, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) departed Beirut for her 12th war patrol (11th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in the western Aegean.
She was to pass the Kaso Straits P.M./12, thence along the north coast of Crete and east coast of Morea to Hyros area to arrive in the Milos area on the 19th.
For the daily positions of HMS Unruly during this patrol see the map below.
12 Feb 1944 In the early hours of the 12th, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) passed the Kaso Strait northbound.

At 2125 B/12, HMS Unruly received the Flag Officer Levent and Eastern Mediterrean (F.O.L.E.M.) his signal timed 1916 B/12 which contained the following orders; Establish patrol on Suda Bay - Milos route. (7)

16 Feb 1944 In a signal timed 2130 A/16, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to the submarines mentioned; 1) RHS Nereus (Lt. A. Panagiotou) proceed to patrol in the vicinity Anaphi and Santorini between long. 25°00'E and 26°00'E. 2) HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) is not to proceed east 24°40'E. (58)

17 Feb 1944 At 0830 B/17, north of Suda Bay, Crete, in position 35°43'N, 24°18'E, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) sighted a small southbound motor caique. The vessel was seen to be armed but Lt. Fyfe decided not to attack it so as not to give way the position of the submarine so early in the patrol.
This was unfortunate as HMS Unruly was ordered to shift patrol to another billet about 36 hours later.
[We have been unable to identify this small caique.] (7)

18 Feb 1944 At 0249 B/18, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) received the Capt. S.1's signal timed 2312 A/17 which contained the following orders; 1) Proceed with all despatch diving by day through the following positions; 'A' = 35°49'N, 23°29'E. 'B' = 35°42'N, 23°16'E at 1700 A/13. 'C' = 35°58'N, 21°56'E. 'D' = 37°40'N, 20°08'E. 2) Proceed at 200 feet between positions 'A' and 'B'. 3) Submerged bombing restrictions arranged to cover speed of advance of 120 miles per day from position 'B'. 4) Further instructions will follow.
At 0651 B/18, HMS Unruly dived to 200 feet to make the westbound passage of the AntiKithera Channel.
At 1853 B/18, HMS Unruly in position 35°37'N, 23°02'E. (59)

19 Feb 1944 In a signal timed 1545 A/19, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to ORP Dzik (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) B.S. Romanowski) and HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN); 1) From position 'D' [we have been unable to find the coordinates of position 'D' for Dzik] in my signal 2105 B/19 Dzik proceed diving by day as follows; 37°40'N, 20°08'E at 1800 Z/22. 39°55'N, 19°13'E at 2302 A/23. thence to patrol as ordered in my signal timed 1550 A/19. 2) From position 'D' (37°40'N, 20°08'E in my signal timed 2312 A/17 Unruly proceed diving by day to position 40°03'N, 19°12'E. E.T.A. 1803 A/21 thence to patrol as in my signal timed 1550 A/19. 3) Submerged bombing restrictions are arranged for both submarines.

In a signal timed 1550 A/19, the following orders were sent to the same two submarines; Establish patrol in area bounded by line joining following positions; 1) Unruly; 'A' = 40°21'N, 19°22'E. 'B' = 40°14'N, 19°22'E. 'C' = 40°02'N, 19°10'E. 'D' = 40°09'N, 19°37'E. 2) Dzik; Positions 'C' and 'D' as for Unruly. 'E' = 39°55'N, 19°10'E. 'F' = 39°55'N, 19°57'E. Paragraph 2 of my signal timed 1545 A/19 refers. 3) Attention is called to Q.B. 68, Q.B 142 and Q.B. 239 (mined areas). 4) Object of your patrol is the destruction of merchant shipping. (58)

21 Feb 1944 At 0620 B/21, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) arrived in her patrol area. Lt. Fyfe decided to patrol 2.5 miles from the coast parallel to it. The object of the patrol was obviously to intercept enemy merchant shipping proceeding between the Adriatic and the Corinth Canal. (7)

23 Feb 1944 In a signal timed 1623 A/23, the Capt. S.1 sent the following information to HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) and ORP Dzik (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) B.S. Romanowski); One enemy submarine possibly in the vicinity of the of Strait Otranto.
[No German U-boat was in that area though.] (58)

2 Mar 1944 At 0701 B/2, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) left patrol to proceed to Malta in accordance with the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1815 A/1. [We have been unable to find the exact text of this signal.] (60)
4 Mar 1944 At 0945 B/4, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) ended her 12th war patrol (11th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She was escorted in. The escort (not identified) had joined at 0715 B/4. (7)
11 Mar 1944 HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) is docked in No.1 graving dock at the Malta Dockyard. (61)
18 Mar 1944 HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) is undocked. (61)
19 Mar 1944 HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) conducted exercises off Malta. (61)

22 Mar 1944 At 1000 B/22, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) departed Malta for her 13th war patrol (12th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to conduct special operation 'Crust' which was a deception landing on Zakynthos to be made by Major Courtney of the SBS (Special Boat Service).
[No patrol report is available for this patrol so details will be missing.]
27 Mar 1944 At 1515 B/7, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) ended her 13th war patrol (12th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.

As for 'Operation Crust', apparently the surf was too strong and the attempt had to be abandoned. Although Unruly made a signal that the operation was "completed as far as possible". [No further details are known to us for the time being.]

The signal was timed 1046 A/26 and contained the following; Operation 'Crust' completed as far as possible but original plan impossible. My position at 1203 A/26, 37°11'N, 18°37'E. E.T.A. outer end Malta N.E. Channel 1506 A/27. (61)
5 Apr 1944 HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) conducted exercises off Malta. (62)
6 Apr 1944 HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) conducted exercises off Malta. (62)
7 Apr 1944 HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) conducted exercises off Malta. (62)
9 Apr 1944 At 1830 B/9, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) departed Malta for her 14th war patrol (13th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in the Aegean.

She was to pass northbound through the Kaso Straits during daylight on the 14th to patrol in an area bounded by; 1) Latitude 35°44'N. 2) Between Longitudes 24°49'N and 26°59'E. 3) N. Coast of Crete. 4) A line drawn 071° Cape Plaka to Kaso.

13 Apr 1944 In a signal timed 0755 B/13, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) reported the following to the Capt. S.1; Ten hours ahead of estimated position. Will pass Kaso tonight. [A weather report was also included.]
In a signal timed 1925 B/13, the Capt. S.1 informed HMS Unruly that an enemy patrol vessel had been reported in Sitia Bay A.M. on this day. (63)

14 Apr 1944 In a signal timed 1544 B/14, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to the submarines mentioned; 1) HMS Unsparing (Lt. A.D. Piper, DSC and 2 bars, RNR) proceed forthwith to patrol the eastern approaches to the Doro channel. 2) HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) proceed with despatch west of Stampalia close east of Liadi and Stapodia to relieve HMS Unsparing east of the Doro channel. 3) Further signal will be made. (64)

15 Apr 1944 In a signal timed 1536 B/15, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to the submarines mentioned; 1) HMS Unsparing (Lt. A.D. Piper, DSC and 2 bars, RNR) leaving patrol at dawn/16 and passing through position 38°01'N, 25°30'E thence through the Kinaros Channel and positions 36°11'N, 25°16'E and 35°49'N, 23°29'E. Diving through the AntiKithera Channel at 200 feet to arrive in position 35°41'N, 23°16'E about dawn/19. 2) HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) is not to cross latitude 37°53'N before 1001 B/16. Then proceeding to patrol eastern approaches to Doro Channel. (64)

16 Apr 1944 In a signal timed 1101 B/16, the Capt. S.1 sent the following to HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN); 1) Bertha (former French wine tanker Bacchus, 1810 GRT, built 1922) believed entered Aegean dawn today destination possibly Piraeus via Doro Channel. Destroyers referred to in the F.O.L.E.M's signal timed 1835 C/15 may be her escort proceeding to the rendezvous off the Dardanelles. 2) You are not to compromise your position by sinking caiques. 3) Anita (former Italian Arezzo, 1165 GRT, built 1899) still present in Leros but not likely to sail yet.

The signal timed 1835 C/15 from F.O.L.E.M. (Flag Officer Levant and Eastern Mediterranean) referred to in the above signal had contained the following; 1) Anita in Porto Lago. Three destroyers north Lipso course (?north). 2) No escort for Anita seen in Porto Lago. 3) She will not sail unescorted. (64)

17 Apr 1944 HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) fires four torpedoes at the German tanker Bertha (former French wine tanker Bacchus, 1810 GRT, built 1922) in the Doro Channel, Greece. All torpedoes fired missed their target.

Bertha was escorted by the German torpedo boats TA 16 (former Italian Castelfidardo), TA 17 (former Italian San Martino) and TA 19 (former Italian Solferino). TA 19 reported seeing three torpedo tracks, one of which passed underneath the stern.
The Bertha had joined the torpedo boats off the entrance to the Dardanelles around 0430 B/16 and arrived at Piraeus around 0630 B/17.
2030 B/16 - Arrived in the intended patrol position, 090° - Cape Doro - 5 miles.
2316 B/16 - Heard HE bearing 340°. Altered course to the westward in an attempt to get between the land and the target.
2321 B/16 - Sighted three darkened ships. Altered course towards to attack.
2325 B/16 - Unruly was on the port bow of all three ships which were seen to be Bertha and two destroyers, one on either bow. Continued the attack.
0020 B/17 - Fired four torpedoes in position 38°02'N, 24°39'E. On firing altered course to 360 degrees and dived. No hits were obtained.
0039 B/17 - Heard an explosion.
0049 B/17 - Heard another explosion.
0052 B/17 - Surfaced. Sighted numerous bursts of tracer ammunition coming from the convoy. Withdrew to the northward.
0503 B/17 - Dived in position 38°13'N, 24°42'E.
1035 B/17 - Sighted a small caique bearing 260° at a range of 8000 yards proceeding on course 080°. Allowed it to proceed unmolested.
1122 B/17 - Sighted a small caique bearing 320° at a range of 16000 yards proceeding on course 180°. Allowed it to proceed unmolested.
1215 B/17 - Sighted a small caique bearing 210° at a range of 9 miles. Course 060° at a speed of 6 knots. Allowed it to proceed unmolested.
2010 B/17 - Surfaced in position 38°08'N, 24°41'E.

2026 B/17 - Received the Capt. S.1's signal tomed 1610 B/17 which contained the following; Bertha arrived Salamis this morning. Anita (former Italian Arezzo, 1165 GRT, built 1899) still present at Porto Lago [Leros].

Lt. Fyfe decided to retire to the south-east to pass his signal timed 0054 B/18 which was done at 0122 B/18. If the presence of HMS Unruly was suspected by the enemy the passing of this signal away from the area they may get the impression that the submarine had left the Doro Channel area.

The signal 0054 B/18 contained the following; Attacked Bertha unsuccessfully at 0030 B/17 in the Doro Channel. Patrol may not have been compromised. My position 37°48'N, 25°21'E [A different position is mentioned in the war diary summaries but this must be a mistake] returning to Doro. (65)
18 Apr 1944 At 0252 B/18, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) sighted a small caique close inshore off Andros, bearing 240° at a range of 5000 yards on a course of 160°.
At 0506 B/18, HMS Unruly dived in position 38°07'N, 24°41'E.
At 1525 B/18, HMS Unruly sighted a large caique bearing 160° at a range of 6000 yards. Course of the caique was 230°.
At 1630 B/18, another large caique was seen bearing 240° at a range of 6000 yards. Course of the caique was 000°.
At 2016 B/18, HMS Unique surfaced in position 38°02'N, 24°43'E.
At 2235 B/18, a small caique eastbound caique was seen. (66)

19 Apr 1944 HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) sank two Greek caiques with gunfire and scuttling charges near Psara, Greece. The first caique sunk was not identified. The second caique sunk was the SYR 591 / Agios Ioannis (38 GRT).

(All times are zone -2) 0027 B/19 - Received the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1840 B/18 which contained the following; 1) Proceed now to patrol in the Gulf of Izmir. 2) Object destruction of Khios - Mitylene - Lemnos traffic. 3) None of our own surface forces are operating in that area. 4) Anita (former Italian Arezzo, 1165 GRT, built 1899) is still in Port Laki [Leros].

Lt. Fyfe set course to pass to the south of Psara. It was thought that the small caique seen at 2235 B/18 was proceeding towards Chios and was most likely going to pass south of Psara. Now being away from the Doro Channel the caique could be attacked.
0204 B/19 - Sighted the caique, closed up the gun crew.

0214 B/19 - In position 38°12'N, 25°00'E opened fire from 1000 yards range. The target was estimated to be of about 30 tons. Five rounds had been fired before the target sank by the bows having been hit three times. The crew of the caique had abandoned ship and were taken alongside with their dinghy. One was wounded and was taken care off. They were also given fresh water and a loaf of bread before they were left to their own devices. Unruly then set course for Khios.
0515 B/19 - When just about to dive sighted a caique to the eastward. Started closing on the surface at full speed.
0545 B/19 - Boarded the caique in position 38°26'N, 25°32'E. The crew had abandoned ship by this time. A demolition charge was placed on board this caique.
0600 B/19 - The charge exploded and the caique sank instantaneously by the stern. Unruly then set course for the Gulf of Izmir.
2005 B/19 - Surfaced in position 38°38'N, 26°04'E and patrolled between Khios and Mitylene during the night.

2031 B/19 - Received the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1515 B/19 which contained the following orders; Leave patrol at dark/21 routed through the Kinaros Channel thence south Santorini to arrive in position 35°49'N, 23°29'E at 1500 B/24th thence under Q.B. 240 (mined area) to position 35°41'N, 23°16'E at 2100 B/24. (63)
20 Apr 1944 At 0439 B/20, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) dived in position 38°44'N, 26°18'E.
At 1710 B/20, in position 38°45'N, 26°21'E, a large northbound caique was seen proceeding northwards close inshore. On investigating this caique a bit further after closing it was seen to be Turkish.
At 2015 B/20, HMS Unruly surfaced in position 38°44'N, 26°10'E. (7)
21 Apr 1944 HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) sank the Greek caique KAL 98 / Agios Nikolaos (46 GRT) with scuttling charges north of Khios Island, Greece.
(All times are zone -2) 0507 B/21 - Dived in position 38°38'N, 26°10'E and closed Port Marmaro which was subsequently seen to be clear of shipping.
1010 B/21 - In position 38°35'N, 26°07'E sighted a caique bearing 335°, range 8 nautical miles. Closed.

1119 B/21 - Surfaced in position 38°37'N, 26°02'E and engaged the caique from a range of 3000 yards. On closing it was seen that she was flying the red cross flag above the Greek ensign and had large red crosses on either bow. Decided to board her for examination.
1140 B/21 - Boarded. Her cargo consisted of oil in drums and a Nazi ensign was found under the Captains pillow. Decided to sink her with a demolition charge.
1150 B/21 - The charge exploded and the caique sank.
1155 B/21 - Dived and withdrew to the westward.
1800 B/21 - Left the area and set course for the Kinaros Channel.
2010 B/21 - Surfaced in position 38°29'N, 25°47'E. (7)
22 Apr 1944 At 0500 B/22, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) dived in position 37°15'N, 26°06'E.

At 1257 B/22, the submarine dived to 200 feet to pass through the Kinaros Channel mine barrage. The M.D.U. was out of action but mines were detected by ordinary hand transmissions while passing through the Channel. HMS Unruly remained deep until 2045 B/25.
At 2055 B/22, HMS Unruly surfaced in position 36°48'N, 26°08'E.

At 2244 B/22, when in position 36°40'N, 25°55'E the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1312 B/22 was received in which orders were received to patrol the western approaches to the Furni Pass by 2100 hours. This signal was not received when first transmitted as HMS Unruly was at 200 feet at that time.
Course was set for the Furni Pass.

At 2256 B/22, the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1625 B/22 was received which stated that the German transport Agathe (former French Kerkena, 1579 GRT, built 1922) was ready to sail from Piraeus this day. (63)

23 Apr 1944 At 0016 B/23, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) passed her signal timed 2304 B/22 in which Lt. Fyfe reported to the Capt. S.1 that HMS Unruly would be unable to reach the position ordered in the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1312 B/22 before 0900 B/23.

At 0050 B/23, the Capt. S.1's signal timed 2225 B/22 was received which contained the following orders; 1) Cancel my 1312 B/22. 2) Leave patrol at dawn/23 routed as in my signal timed 1515 B/19 to arrive in position 35°49'N, 23°29'E at 1500 B/25 thence under Q.B. 240 (mined area) to arrive in position 35°41'N, 23°16'E about 2100 B/25.

0447 B/23, when in position 36°22'N, 25°36'E, the Capt. S.1's signal timed 0210 B/23 was received which contained the following orders; Cancel my signal timed 2225 B/22. Proceed forthwith to cover the eastern approaches to the Doro Channel. Be in position by 1201 B/24.
At 0450 B/23, HMS Unruly dived and altered course to the eastward to comply.
At 1125 B/23, HMS Unruly surfaced when outside visibility distance from Anaphi and ran on the surface.

At 1428 B/23, HMS Unruly passed her signal timed 1321 B/23 in which Lt. Fyfe reported the following to the Capt. S.1; Following minefields plotted by M.D.U. in Kinaros Channel. A) On a line 060° from a position 064° - Cape Prosino - 5.5 miles. B) On a line 230° for 5 miles from position 093° - Cape Prosino - 8 miles. My position is 36°50'N, 26°10'E proceeding to the Doro area. [A weather report was aslo included.]
At 1517 B/23, in position 36°51'N, 26°11'E, HMS Unruly dived to 200 feet to pass through the Kinaros Channel.
At 1746 B/23, having completed the passage, HMS Unruly surfaced in position 37°00'N, 26°09'E, and set course for the Doro Channel.

At 1935 B/23, The signal timed 1931 C/23 from the Flag Officer Levant and Eastern Mediterranean (F.O.L.E.M.) was received which contained the following; Reconnaissance Porto Lago A.M./23 showed the following; l) The German transport Anita (former Italian Arezzo, 1165 GRT, built 1899) still present. 2) The German transport Agathe (former French Kerkena, 1579 GRT, built 1922) and three UJ boats have arrived.

Lt. Fyfe considered it probable that Anita would sail for Piraeus tonight and that it was important to arrive in position to the east of the Doro Chsannel as early as possible so speed was increased to 12 knots and the zig-zag courses were stopped. (67)
24 Apr 1944 At 0300 B/24, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) arrived off the Doro Channel and commenced to patrol in the vicinity of a position 3.8 miles to the north of Cape Kabanos, Andros.
At 0445 B/24, HMS Unruly dived in position 38°06.5'N, 24°45'E.
At 1304 B/24, the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1232 B/24 was received which contained the following; Anita (former Italian Arezzo, built 1899) departed Porto Lago. My signal timed 0210 B/23 refers.
[Anita had departed Porto Lago around 2000 B/23 and had arrived at Piraeus shortly after 1900 B/24. Escorts were UJ 2105 and UJ 2110. They had not proceeded through the Doro Channel.

At 1736 B/24, the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1254 B/23 was received which contained the following orders; 1) Leave patrol at dark today. From position 35°41'N, 23°15'E about 1400 B/27 proceed to make rendezvous with escort in position 35°49'N, 15°18'E at 0530 B/30, thence to Malta. 2) You are to dive by day east of long. 20°00'E. Your surface speed is estimated as 8.5 knots.
At 2003 B/24, HMS Unruly surfaced in position 38°03'N, 24°51'E and set course for Kinaros.

At 2132 B/24, the Capt. S.1's signal timed 2020 B/24 was received which contained the following orders; Cancel my signals 1254 B/24 and 1256 B/24. Resume patrol off the Doro Channel. A 3000 tons merchant ship is expected to sail from Piraeus for Salonika at 2000 B/24, unescorted.
Course was set to proceed to a patrol position 5 miles south of Cape Doro, keeping two miles from the shore. (63)

25 Apr 1944 At 0150 B/25, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) received the Capt. S.1's signal timed 2250 B/24 which contained the following orders; Leave patrol at 0800 B/25 through the Kinaros Channel passing through the following positions; 'A' = 36°10'N, 25°18'E at 0200 B/27. 'B' = 36°10'N, 24°30'E at 1201 B/27. 'C' = 35°49'N, 23°30'E at 0300 B/28. 'D' = 35°41'N, 23°15'E at about 0900 B/28.

At follow up signal timed 2301 B/24 followed which contained the following; My signal 1256 B/24 cancelled. 1) From position 35°41'N, 23°15'E about 0900 B/28 proceed to make rendezvous with the escort in position 35°49'N, 15°18'E at 0600 B/1. 2) You are to dive by day east of long. 20°00'E. Your surface speed is estimated as 8 knots.

At 0447 B/25, HMS Unruly dived in position 38°07'N, 24°40'E. Lt. Fyfe decided to examine Port Kastro on the return passage. Course was set to proceed there at 0800 B/25 as had been ordered as the time to leave patrol.

At 1800 B/25, the masts of two two-masted caiques and probably one single-masted caique were seen in Port Kastro. They were moored in the north-east corner of the harbour and could only be attacked from inside the breakwater.

At 1935 B/25, HMS Unruly surfaced in position 37°52'N, 24°59'E and manoeuvred to get inside the breakwater. However it became apparent that the ships could not be seen clearly enough through the gun telescope and the attack was broken off and HMS Unruly withdrew to seaward and course for the Kinaros Channel. (63)

26 Apr 1944 In a signal timed 1958 B/26, the Capt. S.1 sent the following to HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN); 1) Amend time of the rendezvous as in paragraph 1 of my signal timed 2301 B/26 to read 1402 B/30. 2) Cancel paragraph 2 and amend to read, proceed diving by day on the 28th thence on surface at 8.5 knots. (64)
30 Apr 1944 Around 1815 B/30, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) ended her 14th war patrol (13th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She was escorted in. The escort (not identified) had joined around 1400 B/30. (7)

15 May 1944 At 1800 B/15, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) departed Malta for her 15th war patrol (14th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol north of Crete and to conduct a special operation if an opportunity arose. (Operation 'Portcullis', a Folbot party of limpeteers under Capt. W.P. Gordon, R.M.) was to be send in Candia harbour should a suitable target be present there.
After passing the AntiKithera Channel HMS Unruly was to proceed through positions 36°01'N, 23°50'E and 36° 01'N, 24°40'E to patrol in the vicinity of Candia east of 24°45'E.
[No log is available for this period, therefore no map can be displayed as no daily positions are given in the patrol report.] (68)
17 May 1944 In a signal timed 2331 B/17, the Capt. S.1 ordered HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) to proceed direct to her patrol area after passing the AntiKithera Channel. (69)
18 May 1944 At 0255 B/18, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) entered the Aegean having passed through the AntiKithera Channel.
At 0437 B/18, the submarine dived in position 35°43'N, 23°50'E.
At 2000 B/18, the submarine surfaced in position 35°36'N, 24°38'E.

At 2245 B/18, the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1245 B/18 was received which stated the German transports Anita (former Italian Arezzo, (former Italian Arezzo, 1165 GRT, built 1899), Sabine (former Italian Salvatore, 2281 GRT, built 1921), Gertrud (former Danish Gerda Toft, 1960 GRT, built 1930), Tanais (former Greek, 1545 GRT, built 1907) and Suzanne (former Spanish San Juan II, 552 GRT, built 1919) were all loaded and ready to sail from Piraeus for for Crete or the Dodecanese. (7)
19 May 1944 At 0100 B/18, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) arrived in the vicinity of Candia.

At 0429 B/18, the submarine dived to the north of Candia in position 35°28'N, 25°09'E. The harbour was closed to the 100 fathom line from where it could be seen that there was no shipping of any size in the harbour there.
At 0820 B/18, a northbound caique was seen. It was a small one of about 25 tons.
At 2010 B/18, HMS Unruly surfaced in position 35°25'N, 25°06'E. She then withdrew to the northward.

At 2032 B/18, the Flag Officer Levant and Eastern Mediterranean's (F.O.L.E.M.) signal timed 1817 C/19 was received which stated that reconaissance of Candia at 1150 C/19 had showed 8 caiques, 2 tugs, 4 launches, 1 F-boat and 1 salvage vessel present inside the harbour.
These vessels were all to small for operation 'Portcullis' to be carried out.
Faint HE was heard twice during the night but no contact was made. It is thought the HE may have been from small patrol vessels. (7)

20 May 1944 In a signal timed 2012 B/20, the Capt. S.1's signal timed 2012 B/20 was received which stated HMS Vampire (Lt. C.W. Taylor, RNR), HMS Unsparing (Lt. A.D. Piper, DSC and 2 bars, RNR) and HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) of the following;Sabine (former Italian Salvatore, 2281 GRT, built 1921), Gertrud (former Danish Gerda Toft, 1960 GRT, built 1930) and Tanais (former Greek, 1545 GRT, built 1907) at Salamis, departure imminent. First two almost certainly destined for Crete. Destination of the third not yet known. (69)

20 May 1944 At 0428 B/20, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) dived in position 35°30'N, 25°10'E and closed position 'A'. This was a position chosen by Lt. Fyfe as the most favourable one to intercept southbound shipping. From this position he considered it possible to attack shipping route either direct from Milos or via Santorini. Position 'A' was in 35°25.5'N, 25°07'E.

At 1050 B/20, in position 35°25'N, 25°08'E, a small ship was seen at long range. It was thought to be an F-lighter but it was difficult to make out and thus confirm. It looked like the enemy was sweeping the eastern approach to Candia. Also an armed A/S caique of about 200 tons was seen patrolling the north-west channel during the afternoon. This vessel was transmitting on Asdic and proceeded at a very slow speed of around 1 knot while doing so. [This may have been UJ 2142. which was conducting an A/S sweep between Suda Bay and the western approaches to Candia.]
At 2015 B/20, HMS Unruly surfaced in position 35°25'N, 25°07'E and patrolled to the northward during the night.
A report received of air reconnaissance of Candia showed that the F-lighter had left during the day.
At 2319 B/20, the Capt. S.1's signal timed 2012 B/20 was received. For the text of this signal see the other 'event' for 20 May 1944. (70)

21 May 1944 At 0335 B/21, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) sighted what is thought to be a motor torpedo boat bearing 320° proceeding on course 100°. The submarine turned stern on and withdrew at slow speed to avoid being sighted.

At 0410 B/21, four dark shapes were seen bearing 270° at a range of 8000 yards. Lt. Fyfe assumed these to be three motor torpedo boats and as HMS Unruly was silhouetted against the eastern horizon, he dived. Periscope visibility was not good to the westward but it could be seen that one silhouette was a bit larger that the other three. All were making for Candia and at the time Lt. Fyfe considered the enemy were an F-boat escorted by the motor torpedo boats.
This must have been the F-boat F 123 which arrived at Candia around 0500 B/21 from Suda Bay. She was escorted by the auxiliary patrol vessels GK 01, GK 60 and GK 91.
For the remainder of the day HMS Unruly patrolled in the vicinity of position 'A' (35°25.5'N, 25°07'E).
At 0525 B/21, a two-masted A/S caique mounting three guns (one of about 75 mm) and transmitting on Asdic was seen patrolling off Candia.
This may have been UJ 2142 (a caique) which entered Candia at dawn coming from Suda Bay.
At 0527 B/21, three Arado float planes approached Candia from the northward and circled in the bay until 0650 C/21.

At 1140 B/21, a loud explosion was heard. It was thought this may have been another British submarine attacking the expected convoy. Lt. Fyfe withdrew to the north-west hoping to effect an interception of the expected convoy in daylight.
At 2021 B/21, HMS Unruly surfaced in position 35°42'N, 24°55'E.
At 2101 B/21, the air reconnaissance report of Candia harbour was received which reported no changes. (71)
22 May 1944 At 0350 B/22, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) sighted what is thought to be a motor torpedo boat some way off on bearing 160°. HMS Unruly turned end on.
This must have been the German auxiliary patrol vessel GK 01.
At 0355 B/22, a large dark shape was seen bearing 220°. HMS Unruly commenced to close at slow speed to identify this target.

At 0401 B/22, the target was identified as a large caique which was stopped and listening. HMS Unruly by this time had closed to about 1000 yards. Lt. Fyfe considered it suicidal to dive on a closing course and therefore altered away under full helm at slow speed on the main motors.
At 0402 B/22, the caique opened fire with a 75mm gun and a heavy automatic machine gun. HMS Unruly dived in position 35°22'N, 25°08'E. The caique dropped one depth charge.

This was the auxiliary submarine chaser UJ 2142 (a caique) which reported the encounter in a signal at 0818 B/22. [Note; the KTB of reported the encounter at 0040 B/22 but this is probably a typing error and should most likely read 0400 B/22.] The caique and motor torpedo boat then passed up either side and hunted several miles away to the north-west while HMS Unruly returned to patrol in the vicinity of position 'A' (35°25.5'N, 25°07'E). At 0640 B/22, an F-lighter escorted by a motor launch type vessel were seen on course 300°. [This may have been F 123 which was making a lot of transport runs between Suda Bay and Candia, vice versa. Unfortunately the KTB of the 15th L-flotilla for the second half of May 1944 is not available so we can not be certain. It was however the only F-boat in the area.] During the day, Candia Bay was patrolled by two or three Arado floatplanes. At 1748 B/22, a motor launch type vessel left Candia and commenced an A/S search while using Asdic down the north-west channel. This vessel returned to harbour around 1915 B/22. At 2021 B/22, HMS Unruly surfaced in position 35°26'N, 25°05'E and patrolled to the northward during the night. Air reconnaissance reports of Candia and Piraeus which were received showed no change in either harbour. (72) 22 May 1944 In a signal timed 1010 B/22, the Capt. S.1 sent the following information / orders to Capt. 3.1 221010 B to HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN); 1) The destroyers Le Malin (Cdr. J.E.C. Hourcade), Le Fantasque (Cdr. L.M.P.A. Sala), Le Terrible (Cdr. P.J.G.M. Lancelot) and HMS Kimberley (Lt.Cdr. J.W. Rylands, RN) will be carrying out an anti-shipping sweep in south Aegean between 2101 B/22 and 0503 B/23 and are instructed not to attack submarines. 2) Do not attack destroyer unless proved to be hostile. 3) From 2301 B/22 until dawn/23 patrol in an area bounded by long. 24°59'E / 25°24'E and lat. 35°33'N / the north coast of Crete. In a signal timed 2215 B/22, the above signal was cancelled. The destroyers would not conduct an anti-shipping sweep during the night. (69) 23 May 1944 At 0053 B/23, in position 35°29'N, 25°05'E, sighted an A/S schooner / caique bearing 240°. Withdrew to the north-west. This was most likely again the auxiliary submarine chaser UJ 2142 which reported sighting a submarine at 0105 B/23. At 0433 B/23, HMS Unruly dived in position 35°36'N, 25°03'E and commenced to close position 'A' (35°25.5'N, 25°07'E). At 1120 B/23, the A/S caique was seen to be patrolling in Candia Bay. A motor launch type vessel was seen patrolling in the north-west channel all day while transmitting on Asdic. As by now several motor launch type vessels had been seen in daylight Lt. Fyfe considered that all sightings of motor torpedo boat made during darkness the past days were actually motor launches. UJ 2142 put into Candia that afternoon to effect repairs to some of her equipment including the 'S-gerät / Asdic'. (71) 29 May 1944 In a signal timed 1126 B/29, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN); Leave patrol P.M./30 to arrive in position 35°40'N, 23°30'E at dawn/31. Thence through the AntiKithera Channel. (69) 30 May 1944 At 2018 B/30, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) surfaced in position 35°31'N, 24°57'E and set course for Malta in accordance with the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1126 B/29. (7) 31 May 1944 HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) sank the small Greek sailing vessel PI 870 / Evangelistria (12 GRT) with a demolition charge east of Anti-Kithera. 0446 B/31 - Dived in position 35°50'N, 23°42'E. Shortly before diving a small caique was sighted to the westward steering a course of 190°. 0524 B/31 - Surfaced and closed up the gun crew. Meanwhile closed the target at speed. 0550 B/31 - Range was now 1500 yards. The crew of the caique abandoned ship in their dinghy. Went alongside the caique, boarded and placed a demolition charge. 0600 B/31 - The caique blew up and sank. The crew was then taken prisoner as the patrol was almost over. Set course to the westward on the surface. 0642 B/31 - Dived in position 35°42'N, 22°58'E and went to 200 feet for passage through the Anti-Kithera Channel. 2022 B/31 - Surfaced in position 35°28'N, 22°58'E and continued the passage to Malta. (7) 2 Jun 1944 At 1750 B/2, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) ended her 15th war patrol (14th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. During the patrol special operation 'Portcullis' had not been carried out. No shipping of substantial size had been reported in Candia harbour. She was escorted in. The escort (not identified) had joined at 1210 B/2. (7) 24 Jun 1944 At 1900 B/24, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) departed Malta for her 16th war patrol (15th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in the Aegean. She was to enter the Aegean diving deep through the AntiKithera Channel. [No log is available for this period, therefore no map can be displayed.] (7) 26 Jun 1944 In a signal timed 1242 B/26 the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to the submarines mentioned; 1) HMS Vampire (Lt. C.W. Taylor, RNR) leave patrol at dusk/28 passing north of Stampalia and west of Anaphi to arrive in position 35°49'N, 23°29'E at dawn/30 thence diving at 200 feet to position 35°4?'N, 23°15'E. Thence as necessary to arrive in position 35°42'N, 22°11'E at 0001 B/1. 2) HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) proceed direct patrol on Rhodes-Leros route between latitudes 36°53'N and 36°30'N westwards of a line 25° from Kandelioussa. Enter your area from southwards at 2202 B/28. (73) 26 Jun 1944 At 2350 B/26, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) entered the Aegean having completed the eastbound passage of the AntiKithera Channel. (7) 28 Jun 1944 At 2300 B/28, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) entered her patrol area in the vicinity of Cape Daphni. The object of the patrol was to intercept the German transports Anita (former Italian Arezzo, 1165 GRT, built 1899) and Agathe (former French Kerkena, 1579 GRT, built 1922), whose sailing from Rhodos to Leros was imminent. However during the three days spent in the Cape Daphni area only three small caiques were sighted which were all allowed to proceed unmolested so as to not give the submarines position away. (7) 30 Jun 1944 In a signal timed 1854 B/30, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to the submarines mentioned; 1) At dusk/1 HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) proceed west of Kinaros to patrol in the north Aegean between lats. 39°30'N and 37°45'E and east of long. 25°30'E. 2) From dawn/2 HMS Vigorous (Lt. J.C. Ogle, DSC, RN) keep outside HMS Unruly's area. (73) 1 Jul 1944 At 2000 B/1, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) left the Cape Daphni area in accordance with the Capt. S.1 signal timed 1854 B/30. [See the 'event' for 30 June for the text of this signal.] At 2151 B/1, the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1847 B/1 was received which contained the following orders; My signal timed 1732 B/1. 1) Pass through the Kinaros Channel and patrol in position 37°25'N, 26°08'E 2) Force 133 reports the small German tanker Dresden (120 GRT, built ????) and the small German transport Suzanne (former Spanish San Juan II, 552 GRT, built 1919) left Georgios for Rhodes A.M./today. 3) Suzanne, one corvette, two Danube barges, two launches arrived Syros at 0915 B/today. Will probably sail for Leros P.M./today. [In the signal the Capt. S.1 referred to his signal timed 1732 B/1. We have been unable to find the text of this signal.] Lt. Fyfe ordered speed increased to full to try to intercept the convoy referred to in paragraph 3 but there was little hope this would be successful. (74) 2 Jul 1944 At 0430 B/2, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) arrived in the ordered position (37°25'N, 26°08'E) having passed through the Kinaros Channel at 200 feet. No mine contacts were obtained by the M.D.U. (Mine Detection Unit) in the channel. At 2255 B/2, the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1910 B/2 was received which contained the following orders; Patrol in the eastern Approaches to the Steno Pass by dawn/3. Course was set accordingly. (74) 3 Jul 1944 HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) fires three torpedoes at the German transport KT 18 / Pelikan (834 GRT, built 1944) north-east of Mykonos, Greece. The torpedoes however miss their target. Pelikan was escorted by the German motor minesweeper R 185. The convoy had departed Piraeus for Porto Lago (Leros) around 1600 B/2. Originally the German transport KT 26 / Erpel (834 GRT, built 1944) had also been part of the convoy but she returned to Piraeus around 1630 B/2 with engine trouble. The Germans reported that, at 0210 B/3, about 12 miles to the north-east of Mykonos, two explosions which threw up huge columns of water, one in front of the Pelikan and one astern. Pelikan turned away and opened fire with a machine gun soon followed by the stern 75mm gun. No torpedo tracks had been sighted. The convoy, which ultimate destination was Rhodos, arrived at Porto Lago, Leros around 0800 B/3. 0140 B/3 - In position 37°37'N, 25°31'E the Officer of the Watch sighted what he thought was smoke bearing 330° and about four minutes later this was confirmed by the bright red glow from a ships funnel on the same bearing. On closing the target was seen to be a KT-ship escorted by an 'E'-bost or 'R'-boat. 0156 B/3 - Fired three torpedoes from 1000 yards at the KT ship. No hits were obtained as the enemy altered course after the last torpedo had been fired. It was however thought at that time that the second torpedo had hit the target but this was not the case. 0218 B/3 - Came to periscope depth. Nothing in sight. 0230 B/3 - Surfaced and set course for the Steno Pass. 0430 B/3 - Dived in the eastern approaches to the Steno Pass. Patrolled in that area until 5 July to try to intercept the German transport Anita (former Italian Arezzo, 1165 GRT, built 1899) which had now been reported to be at Leros. (75) 5 Jul 1944 At 1527 B/5, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) received the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1307 B/5 which ordered her to patrol in the eastern approaches to the Doro Channel by 0100 B/6. At 2040 B/5, HMS Unruly surfaced in position 37°44'N, 25°07'E and set course for the Doro Channel. At 2202 B/5, when in position 37°52'N, 25°07'E, the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1816 B/5 was received which ordered HMS Unruly to patrol in position 259° - Position 'BB' = 6 miles by 0200 B/6. [Unknown to us at this moment is where position 'BB' exactly was.] (7) 6 Jul 1944 At 0050 B/6, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) arrived in position 259° - Position 'BB' - 6 miles as had been ordered in the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1816 B/5. At 0246 B/6, the Capt. S.1's signal timed 0137 B/6 was received in which the patrol position was changed to 220° - Position 'BB' - 10 miles. At 0340 B/6, HMS Unruly arrived in the newly ordered position. At 0350 B/6, an aircraft enemy report was received (Time Of Origin 0001 Z/6) of a convoy of two merchant vessels, one destroyer and four other unknown vessel in position 37°44'N, 26°00'E, course 240°, speed 7 knots. Lt. Fyfe decided to try to intercept this convoy in position 37°34'N, 25°38'E at 0500 B/6. It was possible to arrive in this position by proceeding at full speed on the surface. At 0455 B/6, HMS Unruly dived in the selected position. By 0641 B/6, nothing had been sighted so HMS Unruly surfsaced and set course for the ordered patrol position. At 0727 B/6, HMS Unruly dived in position 37°45'N, 25°37'E. At 1148 B/6, the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1004 B/6 was received in which HMS Unruly was ordered to endeavour to reach a position to the north of Cape Daphni by 0100 B/7. Lt. Fyfe surfaced and set course for the Kinaros Channel at full surfaced speed. At 1800 B/6, HMS Unruly dived to 200 feet to pass through the Kinaros Channel. At 2032 B/6, HMS Unruly surfaced in position 36°54'N, 26°18'E and continued at full speed on the surface. At 2130 B/6, HMS Unruly received the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1731 B/6 in which she was ordered to endeavour to reach position 345 - Position 'CC' - 29 miles by 0200 B/7. [We do not know the exact location of position 'CC' but the ordered position was to the north of Kandelioussa.] (7) 7 Jul 1944 At 0007 B/7, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) arrived in position 345° - Position 'CC' - 29 miles and commenced charging the battery. At 0355 B/7, she dived. At 0415 B/7, the Capt. S.1's signal timed 0135 B/7 was received in which she was ordered to examine Megalo Bay at 0500 B/7. The bay was examined in the afternoon but it was found to be clear. Course was set to the westward shortly before 1700 B/7. In a signal timed 1614 B/7, HMS Unruly was ordered to patrol in the vicinity of position 017° - Position 'CC' - 19 miles. (7) 8 Jul 1944 In a signal timed 1021 B/8, the Capt. S.1 ordered HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) to patrol in the vicinity of position, 360° - position 'CC' - 25 miles by 2300 B/8. In a signal timed 1944 B/8, the Capt. S.1 ordered her to resume patrol in position, 017° - Position 'CC' - 19 miles as had earlier been ordered in the signal timed 1614 B/7. (76) 10 Jul 1944 During daylight, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) examined Livadia Bay but nothing was sighted. In a signal timed 1720 B/10, the Capt. S.1 ordered HMS Unruly to patrol in the Kandelioussa - Niseros - Gaidara triangle. Lt. Fyfe decided to patrol 3 miles to the south-east of Rakhia. (74) 11 Jul 1944 HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) attacked the German transport KT 18 / Pelikan (834 GRT, built 1944) south-west of Kos, Greece. The torpedoes however all missed their target. Pelikan was escorted by the German motor minesweepers R 185 and R 211. The convoy was on passage from Rhodos to Leros. The convoy arrived at Porto Lago around 0700 B/11. 0345 B/11 - In position 36°32'N, 27°37'E heard HE bearing 070° and altered course towards. 0358 B/11 - Sighted the red funnel glow from a ships funnel some way off. Set course to intercept the enemy. [The red funnel glow was the telltale sign of a German KT ship.] 0402 B/11 - Identified the target as a KT-ship escorted by two E-boats or R-boats, one ahead and one astern. 0422 B/11 - Dived as it was now becoming very light. Range to the enemy on diving was 3000 yards. 0426 B/11 - In position 36°36'N, 27°03'E fired a salvo of four torpedoes from yards. No hits were obtained although a hit was claimed. 0448 B/11 - Sighted the enemy bearing 320° proceeding on a course of approximately 300°. 0450 B/11 - Surfaced in an attempt to ascertain if the target was listed or down by the bow but although the range was only about 7000 yards no details could be made out. 0452 B/11 - Dived. 0529 B/11 - Surfaced to pass Unruly's signal timed 0523 B/11 in which the attack was reported to the Capt. S.1 and also that there was only one torpedo remaining. The signal was passed at 0610 B/11 and five minutes later HMS Unruly dived for the day. 1027 B/11 - Received the Capt. S.1's signal timed 0755 B/11 in which was ordered to leave her present patrol forthwith and patrol to the south of Milos. This signal also estimated Unruly's diving position at dawn/12 as south of Christiana. It was therefore possible to examine Livadia Bay and still reach this diving position by proceeding on the surface in daylight. 1433 B/11 - It was seen that Livadia Bay was clear, HMS Unruly surfaced and set course for the Milos area. 1534 B/11 - The Capt. S.1's signal timed 1204 B/11 was received in which his signal timed 0755 B/11 was cancelled. Unruly was now ordered to patrol on the Santorini - Candia route by dawn/12. The object of this patrol was to intercept a caique convoy escorted by four small vessels which had been reported by air reconnaissance to be present in Milos. (75) 12 Jul 1944 At 0417 B/12, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) dived in position 36°06'N, 25°19'E on the Santorini - Candia route. At 1438 B/12, the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1020 B/12 was received which contained the following orders; Leave patrol at 0801 B/13 to carry out an anti-caique sweep from Candia to Cape Spada so as to arrive in position 35°49'N, 23°29'E at 0401 B/14 thence through Anti Kithera Channel to return to Malta. At 2050 B/12, HMS Unruly surfaced in position 36°08'N, 26°16'E and maintained a surface patrol in this position until midnight. It was hoped to intercept the expected caique convoy before moonrise. (74) 13 Jul 1944 At 0430 B/13, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) dived off Candia and patrolled in the area until noon. At 1200 B/13, course was set for the AntiKithera Channel keeping close to the north coast of Crete. At 2021 B/13, HMS Unruly surfaced in position 35°32'N, 24°48'E. In a signal timed 1054 B/13, from the Capt. S.1 the following orders had been received; From position 35°41'N, 23°16'E about 1100 B/14 proceed dived and on the surface at 9 knots as requisite capable to rendezvous with the escort in position 35°49'N, 15°18'E at 0915 B/16. (74) 14 Jul 1944 HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) made the westbound passage through the AntiKithera Channel. Just before diving a southbound caique was sighted 3 miles to the westward but the vessel was so small that it was not considered worth to molest it. (7) 16 Jul 1944 At 1200 B/16, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) ended her 16th war patrol (15th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. This had been her last war patrol. She was now to return to the U.K. to refit. (7) 24 Jul 1944 Convoy GUS 47. Donald (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), John Ireland (American, 7247 GRT, built 1944), Kong Sverre (Norwegian, 7238 GRT, built 1941), Lawrence D. Tyson (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), Lidvard (Norwegian, 4785 GRT, built 1939), Luminetta (British (tanker), 6159 GRT, built 1927), Polartank (Norwegian (tanker), 6356 GRT, built 1930), Samblade (British, 7219 GRT, built 1943), Samglory (British, 7210 GRT, built 1944), Samkey (British, 7219 GRT, built 1943), Samleyte (British, 7255 GRT, built 1944), Tricolor (Norwegian, 6850 GRT, built 1933) and William W. She rejoined the convoy at 1715 Q/13. USS Decker had been detached to stand by the stricken vessel. At 1230 Q/13, fuelling operations were completed. All thirteen escorts were fuelled on this day. The Chesapeake Bay section proceeded to it's destination escorted by Escort Division 5 made up of USS Evarts, USS Wyffels, USS Decker, USS Dobler and USS Walter S. Brown. The Chesapeake Bay section was released off it's destination around 2300 Q/17 after which the escorts set course for the Boston Navy Yard. The remainder of the convoy proceeded by New York with the remaining escorts. At 0800 Z/2, in position 14°44'N, 18°00'W, the following merchant vessels joined the convoy coming from Dakar; John Laurance (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942) and Marrakech (French, 6179 GRT, built 1914). At 0945 Z/4, in position 21.14'N, 18.01'W, the John Laurance parted company to proceed to Port Etienne to due to one of the crew being seriously ill. At 0900 Z/10, in position 35°15'N, 10°28'W, the Fauzan and Marrakech were detached to Casablanca. Around 1030 Z/10, in position 35°18'N, 10°28'W, the following merchant vessels joined the convoy coming from Casablanca; Cap Cantin (British (former French), 3317 GRT, built 1933), Fort Ellice (British, 7129 GRT, built 1942) and Porjus (Swedish, 2965 GRT, built 1906). These ships were escorted from Casablanca to the rendezvous by the minesweeping sloop Gazelle (?) and the patrol vessel L'Effronté (?) which subsequently escorted the two merchant vessels that had parted company with the convoy to Casablanca where they arrived on the 11th. At the same time as the Casablanca section joined up convoy MKS 57(G) merged with convoy SL 166 into the combined convoy SL 166 / MKS 57(G). The following merchant vessels made up convoy MKS 57(G) which now thus joined; Baron Douglas (British, 3899 GRT, built 1932), City of Dundee (British, 5273 GRT, built 1921), Clan MacNeil (British, 6111 GRT, built 1922), Cydonia (British, 3517 GRT, built 1927), Edward Bruce (British, 7219 GRT, built 1943), Empire Torrent (British, 7076 GRT, built 1942), Fort Prudhomme (British, 7167 GRT, built 1943), Manchester Division (British, 6048 GRT, built 1918), Marpessa (Dutch (tanker, serving as escort oiler), 7408 GRT, built 1927), Marsdale (British, 4890 GRT, built 1940), Nurani (British, 5414 GRT, built 1941), Ocean Pelgrim (American, 7177 GRT, built 1942), Pierre Soule (American, 7191 GRT, built 1943), Sarpedon (British, 11321 GRT, built 1923) and Sheaf Crown (British, 4868 GRT, built 1929). Piper, DSC and 2 bars, RNR) also joined the convoy. The escort of convoy KMS 57(G) took over the escort of the now combined convoy and HMS Enchantress and HMS Ness proceeded to Gibraltar arriving there on the 11th. The new escort was made up of the frigates HMS Helmsdale (Cdr. C.W. McMullen, DSC, RN), HMS Foley (A/Lt.Cdr. C.A.H. Bird, RNVR) and the corvettes HMS Kenilworth Castle (Lt. J.J.Allon, RNR), HMS Launceston Castle (Lt. R.M. Roberts, RNR), HMS Pevensey Castle (A/Lt.Cdr. C.W. Leadbetter, RNR) and HMS Portchester Castle (Lt. A.G. Scott, RNR). At the time the convoys merged the destroyer HMS Highlander (Cdr. D.A. Rayner, DSC, VRD, RNVR) also joined the convoy escort. At 1050 Z/10, in position 35°18'N, 10°28'W, the Norwegian was detached by the Commodore to proceed to Casablanca due to a defective boiler. HMS Kilbirnie was given the task to escort her there. They arrived at Casablanca on the 11th. At 1000 Z/11, in position 36°00'N, 14°18'W, the escort carrier HMS Campania (A/Capt. K.A. Bourke, RNZNR) joined the convoy. At 1400 Z/12, in position 38°38'N, 18°00'W, the destroyer HMS Antelope (Cdr. J.G. Gould, RN) joined the convoy. At 2000 Z/15, in position 48°52'N, 18°20'W, the Marpesa parted company with the convoy to join convoy OS 86. At 0730 Z/16, in position 50°20'N, 17°54'W, the following merchant vessels of the convoy parted company to proceed ahead to the U.K. as fast section; Bosphorus, Greystoke Castle, Henri Jaspar and Marsdale. They took HMS Campania, HMS Antelope, HMS Foley and HMS HMS Portchester Castle with them as escorts. The fast section was dispersed at 0230 B/18 in the North Channel. At 0700 B/18, HMS Helmsdale, HMS Kenilworth Castle, HMS Launceston Castle and HMS Pevensey Castle were detached to Londonderry where they arrived later the same day. At 0740 B/18, the following merchant vessels were detached to proceed towards the North Channel. This were the Clyde, Mersey, Belfast and Bristol Channel sections of the convoy. The Clyde section was made up of the following merchant vessels; City of Dundee, Cydonia, Pierre Soule and Zypenberg. The rescue ship Goodwin also went to the Clyde with them. The Mersey section was made up of the following merchant vessels; Bothnia, Calgary, Clan MacNeil, Edward Bruce, Empire Torrent, Fort Cadotte, Fort Panmure and Ittersum. The Belfast section was made up of only one merchant vessel, the Porjus. The Bristol Channel section was made up of the following merchant vessels; Fort Ellice and Ocean Pilgrim. HMS Unruly and HMS Unsparing also parted company to proceed with the North Channel section. At 1615 B/18, they parted company with the North Channel section to proceed to Dundee escorted by HMS Cutty Sark (Lt. H.J. Bartlett, DSC, RN). The North Channel section was escorted by HMS Highlander which arrived at Londonderry on the 19th. At 1200 B/19, the following merchant vessels arrived at Loch Ewe for onward routing to east coast ports; Baron Douglas, Cap Cantin, Fort de Troyon, Fort Paskoyac, Fort Prudhomme, Manchester Division, Northleigh, Nurani, Sarpedon, Sheaf Crown, Wearwood and Yearby.
