The caique and motor torpedo boat then passed up either side and hunted several miles away to the north-west while HMS Unruly returned to patrol in the vicinity of position 'A' (35°25.5'N, 25°07'E).
At 0640 B/22, an F-lighter escorted by a motor launch type vessel were seen on course 300°.

[This may have been F 123 which was making a lot of transport runs between Suda Bay and Candia, vice versa. Unfortunately the KTB of the 15th L-flotilla for the second half of May 1944 is not available so we can not be certain. It was however the only F-boat in the area.]
During the day, Candia Bay was patrolled by two or three Arado floatplanes.
At 1748 B/22, a motor launch type vessel left Candia and commenced an A/S search while using Asdic down the north-west channel. This vessel returned to harbour around 1915 B/22.
At 2021 B/22, HMS Unruly surfaced in position 35°26'N, 25°05'E and patrolled to the northward during the night.
Air reconnaissance reports of Candia and Piraeus which were received showed no change in either harbour. (72)

22 May 1944 In a signal timed 1010 B/22, the Capt. S.1 sent the following information / orders to Capt. 3.1 221010 B to HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN); 1) The destroyers Le Malin (Cdr. J.E.C. Hourcade), Le Fantasque (Cdr. L.M.P.A. Sala), Le Terrible (Cdr. P.J.G.M. Lancelot) and HMS Kimberley (Lt.Cdr. J.W. Rylands, RN) will be carrying out an anti-shipping sweep in south Aegean between 2101 B/22 and 0503 B/23 and are instructed not to attack submarines. 2) Do not attack destroyer unless proved to be hostile. 3) From 2301 B/22 until dawn/23 patrol in an area bounded by long. 24°59'E / 25°24'E and lat. 35°33'N / the north coast of Crete.
In a signal timed 2215 B/22, the above signal was cancelled. The destroyers would not conduct an anti-shipping sweep during the night. (69)
23 May 1944 At 0053 B/23, in position 35°29'N, 25°05'E, sighted an A/S schooner / caique bearing 240°. Withdrew to the north-west.
This was most likely again the auxiliary submarine chaser UJ 2142 which reported sighting a submarine at 0105 B/23.
At 0433 B/23, HMS Unruly dived in position 35°36'N, 25°03'E and commenced to close position 'A' (35°25.5'N, 25°07'E).

At 1120 B/23, the A/S caique was seen to be patrolling in Candia Bay. A motor launch type vessel was seen patrolling in the north-west channel all day while transmitting on Asdic. As by now several motor launch type vessels had been seen in daylight Lt. Fyfe considered that all sightings of motor torpedo boat made during darkness the past days were actually motor launches.
UJ 2142 put into Candia that afternoon to effect repairs to some of her equipment including the 'S-gerät / Asdic'. (71)

29 May 1944 In a signal timed 1126 B/29, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN); Leave patrol P.M./30 to arrive in position 35°40'N, 23°30'E at dawn/31. Thence through the AntiKithera Channel. (69)
30 May 1944 At 2018 B/30, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) surfaced in position 35°31'N, 24°57'E and set course for Malta in accordance with the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1126 B/29. (7)
31 May 1944 HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) sank the small Greek sailing vessel PI 870 / Evangelistria (12 GRT) with a demolition charge east of Anti-Kithera.
0446 B/31 - Dived in position 35°50'N, 23°42'E. Shortly before diving a small caique was sighted to the westward steering a course of 190°.
0524 B/31 - Surfaced and closed up the gun crew. Meanwhile closed the target at speed.
0550 B/31 - Range was now 1500 yards. The crew of the caique abandoned ship in their dinghy. Went alongside the caique, boarded and placed a demolition charge.
0600 B/31 - The caique blew up and sank. The crew was then taken prisoner as the patrol was almost over. Set course to the westward on the surface.
0642 B/31 - Dived in position 35°42'N, 22°58'E and went to 200 feet for passage through the Anti-Kithera Channel.
2022 B/31 - Surfaced in position 35°28'N, 22°58'E and continued the passage to Malta. (7)
2 Jun 1944 At 1750 B/2, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) ended her 15th war patrol (14th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
During the patrol special operation 'Portcullis' had not been carried out. No shipping of substantial size had been reported in Candia harbour.
She was escorted in. The escort (not identified) had joined at 1210 B/2. (7)
24 Jun 1944 At 1900 B/24, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) departed Malta for her 16th war patrol (15th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in the Aegean.
She was to enter the Aegean diving deep through the AntiKithera Channel.
[No log is available for this period, therefore no map can be displayed.] (7)

26 Jun 1944 In a signal timed 1242 B/26 the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to the submarines mentioned; 1) HMS Vampire (Lt. C.W. Taylor, RNR) leave patrol at dusk/28 passing north of Stampalia and west of Anaphi to arrive in position 35°49'N, 23°29'E at dawn/30 thence diving at 200 feet to position 35°4?'N, 23°15'E. Thence as necessary to arrive in position 35°42'N, 22°11'E at 0001 B/1. 2) HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) proceed direct patrol on Rhodes-Leros route between latitudes 36°53'N and 36°30'N westwards of a line 25° from Kandelioussa. Enter your area from southwards at 2202 B/28. (73)
26 Jun 1944 At 2350 B/26, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) entered the Aegean having completed the eastbound passage of the AntiKithera Channel. (7)
28 Jun 1944 At 2300 B/28, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) entered her patrol area in the vicinity of Cape Daphni.

The object of the patrol was to intercept the German transports Anita (former Italian Arezzo, 1165 GRT, built 1899) and Agathe (former French Kerkena, 1579 GRT, built 1922), whose sailing from Rhodos to Leros was imminent.
However during the three days spent in the Cape Daphni area only three small caiques were sighted which were all allowed to proceed unmolested so as to not give the submarines position away. (7)

30 Jun 1944 In a signal timed 1854 B/30, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to the submarines mentioned; 1) At dusk/1 HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) proceed west of Kinaros to patrol in the north Aegean between lats. 39°30'N and 37°45'E and east of long. 25°30'E. 2) From dawn/2 HMS Vigorous (Lt. J.C. Ogle, DSC, RN) keep outside HMS Unruly's area. (73)
1 Jul 1944 At 2000 B/1, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) left the Cape Daphni area in accordance with the Capt. S.1 signal timed 1854 B/30. [See the 'event' for 30 June for the text of this signal.]

At 2151 B/1, the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1847 B/1 was received which contained the following orders; My signal timed 1732 B/1. 1) Pass through the Kinaros Channel and patrol in position 37°25'N, 26°08'E 2) Force 133 reports the small German tanker Dresden (120 GRT, built ????) and the small German transport Suzanne (former Spanish San Juan II, 552 GRT, built 1919) left Georgios for Rhodes A.M./today. 3) Suzanne, one corvette, two Danube barges, two launches arrived Syros at 0915 B/today. Will probably sail for Leros P.M./today.
[In the signal the Capt. S.1 referred to his signal timed 1732 B/1. We have been unable to find the text of this signal.]
Lt. Fyfe ordered speed increased to full to try to intercept the convoy referred to in paragraph 3 but there was little hope this would be successful. (74)

2 Jul 1944 At 0430 B/2, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) arrived in the ordered position (37°25'N, 26°08'E) having passed through the Kinaros Channel at 200 feet. No mine contacts were obtained by the M.D.U. (Mine Detection Unit) in the channel.
At 2255 B/2, the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1910 B/2 was received which contained the following orders; Patrol in the eastern Approaches to the Steno Pass by dawn/3.
Course was set accordingly. (74)

3 Jul 1944 HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) fires three torpedoes at the German transport KT 18 / Pelikan (834 GRT, built 1944) north-east of Mykonos, Greece. The torpedoes however miss their target. Pelikan was escorted by the German motor minesweeper R 185.

The convoy had departed Piraeus for Porto Lago (Leros) around 1600 B/2. Originally the German transport KT 26 / Erpel (834 GRT, built 1944) had also been part of the convoy but she returned to Piraeus around 1630 B/2 with engine trouble.

The Germans reported that, at 0210 B/3, about 12 miles to the north-east of Mykonos, two explosions which threw up huge columns of water, one in front of the Pelikan and one astern. Pelikan turned away and opened fire with a machine gun soon followed by the stern 75mm gun. No torpedo tracks had been sighted.
The convoy, which ultimate destination was Rhodos, arrived at Porto Lago, Leros around 0800 B/3.

0140 B/3 - In position 37°37'N, 25°31'E the Officer of the Watch sighted what he thought was smoke bearing 330° and about four minutes later this was confirmed by the bright red glow from a ships funnel on the same bearing. On closing the target was seen to be a KT-ship escorted by an 'E'-bost or 'R'-boat.

0156 B/3 - Fired three torpedoes from 1000 yards at the KT ship. No hits were obtained as the enemy altered course after the last torpedo had been fired. It was however thought at that time that the second torpedo had hit the target but this was not the case.
0218 B/3 - Came to periscope depth. Nothing in sight.
0230 B/3 - Surfaced and set course for the Steno Pass.
0430 B/3 - Dived in the eastern approaches to the Steno Pass.
Patrolled in that area until 5 July to try to intercept the German transport Anita (former Italian Arezzo, 1165 GRT, built 1899) which had now been reported to be at Leros. (75)
5 Jul 1944 At 1527 B/5, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) received the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1307 B/5 which ordered her to patrol in the eastern approaches to the Doro Channel by 0100 B/6.
At 2040 B/5, HMS Unruly surfaced in position 37°44'N, 25°07'E and set course for the Doro Channel.

At 2202 B/5, when in position 37°52'N, 25°07'E, the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1816 B/5 was received which ordered HMS Unruly to patrol in position 259° - Position 'BB' = 6 miles by 0200 B/6. [Unknown to us at this moment is where position 'BB' exactly was.] (7)
6 Jul 1944 At 0050 B/6, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) arrived in position 259° - Position 'BB' - 6 miles as had been ordered in the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1816 B/5.
At 0246 B/6, the Capt. S.1's signal timed 0137 B/6 was received in which the patrol position was changed to 220° - Position 'BB' - 10 miles.
At 0340 B/6, HMS Unruly arrived in the newly ordered position.

At 0350 B/6, an aircraft enemy report was received (Time Of Origin 0001 Z/6) of a convoy of two merchant vessels, one destroyer and four other unknown vessel in position 37°44'N, 26°00'E, course 240°, speed 7 knots.
Lt. Fyfe decided to try to intercept this convoy in position 37°34'N, 25°38'E at 0500 B/6. It was possible to arrive in this position by proceeding at full speed on the surface.
At 0455 B/6, HMS Unruly dived in the selected position.
By 0641 B/6, nothing had been sighted so HMS Unruly surfsaced and set course for the ordered patrol position.
At 0727 B/6, HMS Unruly dived in position 37°45'N, 25°37'E.
At 1148 B/6, the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1004 B/6 was received in which HMS Unruly was ordered to endeavour to reach a position to the north of Cape Daphni by 0100 B/7.
Lt. Fyfe surfaced and set course for the Kinaros Channel at full surfaced speed.
At 1800 B/6, HMS Unruly dived to 200 feet to pass through the Kinaros Channel.
At 2032 B/6, HMS Unruly surfaced in position 36°54'N, 26°18'E and continued at full speed on the surface.

At 2130 B/6, HMS Unruly received the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1731 B/6 in which she was ordered to endeavour to reach position 345 - Position 'CC' - 29 miles by 0200 B/7. [We do not know the exact location of position 'CC' but the ordered position was to the north of Kandelioussa.] (7)
7 Jul 1944 At 0007 B/7, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) arrived in position 345° - Position 'CC' - 29 miles and commenced charging the battery.
At 0355 B/7, she dived.
At 0415 B/7, the Capt. S.1's signal timed 0135 B/7 was received in which she was ordered to examine Megalo Bay at 0500 B/7.
The bay was examined in the afternoon but it was found to be clear. Course was set to the westward shortly before 1700 B/7.
In a signal timed 1614 B/7, HMS Unruly was ordered to patrol in the vicinity of position 017° - Position 'CC' - 19 miles. (7)
8 Jul 1944 In a signal timed 1021 B/8, the Capt. S.1 ordered HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) to patrol in the vicinity of position, 360° - position 'CC' - 25 miles by 2300 B/8.
In a signal timed 1944 B/8, the Capt. S.1 ordered her to resume patrol in position, 017° - Position 'CC' - 19 miles as had earlier been ordered in the signal timed 1614 B/7. (76)
10 Jul 1944 During daylight, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) examined Livadia Bay but nothing was sighted.
In a signal timed 1720 B/10, the Capt. S.1 ordered HMS Unruly to patrol in the Kandelioussa - Niseros - Gaidara triangle. Lt. Fyfe decided to patrol 3 miles to the south-east of Rakhia. (74)
11 Jul 1944 HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) attacked the German transport KT 18 / Pelikan (834 GRT, built 1944) south-west of Kos, Greece. The torpedoes however all missed their target.
Pelikan was escorted by the German motor minesweepers R 185 and R 211.
The convoy was on passage from Rhodos to Leros. The convoy arrived at Porto Lago around 0700 B/11.
0345 B/11 - In position 36°32'N, 27°37'E heard HE bearing 070° and altered course towards.
0358 B/11 - Sighted the red funnel glow from a ships funnel some way off. Set course to intercept the enemy. [The red funnel glow was the telltale sign of a German KT ship.]
0402 B/11 - Identified the target as a KT-ship escorted by two E-boats or R-boats, one ahead and one astern.
0422 B/11 - Dived as it was now becoming very light. Range to the enemy on diving was 3000 yards.
0426 B/11 - In position 36°36'N, 27°03'E fired a salvo of four torpedoes from yards. No hits were obtained although a hit was claimed.
0448 B/11 - Sighted the enemy bearing 320° proceeding on a course of approximately 300°.
0450 B/11 - Surfaced in an attempt to ascertain if the target was listed or down by the bow but although the range was only about 7000 yards no details could be made out.
0452 B/11 - Dived.

0529 B/11 - Surfaced to pass Unruly's signal timed 0523 B/11 in which the attack was reported to the Capt. S.1 and also that there was only one torpedo remaining. The signal was passed at 0610 B/11 and five minutes later HMS Unruly dived for the day.

1027 B/11 - Received the Capt. S.1's signal timed 0755 B/11 in which was ordered to leave her present patrol forthwith and patrol to the south of Milos. This signal also estimated Unruly's diving position at dawn/12 as south of Christiana.
It was therefore possible to examine Livadia Bay and still reach this diving position by proceeding on the surface in daylight.
1433 B/11 - It was seen that Livadia Bay was clear, HMS Unruly surfaced and set course for the Milos area.

1534 B/11 - The Capt. S.1's signal timed 1204 B/11 was received in which his signal timed 0755 B/11 was cancelled. Unruly was now ordered to patrol on the Santorini - Candia route by dawn/12. The object of this patrol was to intercept a caique convoy escorted by four small vessels which had been reported by air reconnaissance to be present in Milos. (75)
12 Jul 1944 At 0417 B/12, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) dived in position 36°06'N, 25°19'E on the Santorini - Candia route.

At 1438 B/12, the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1020 B/12 was received which contained the following orders; Leave patrol at 0801 B/13 to carry out an anti-caique sweep from Candia to Cape Spada so as to arrive in position 35°49'N, 23°29'E at 0401 B/14 thence through Anti Kithera Channel to return to Malta.

At 2050 B/12, HMS Unruly surfaced in position 36°08'N, 26°16'E and maintained a surface patrol in this position until midnight. It was hoped to intercept the expected caique convoy before moonrise. (74)
13 Jul 1944 At 0430 B/13, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) dived off Candia and patrolled in the area until noon.
At 1200 B/13, course was set for the AntiKithera Channel keeping close to the north coast of Crete.
At 2021 B/13, HMS Unruly surfaced in position 35°32'N, 24°48'E.

In a signal timed 1054 B/13, from the Capt. S.1 the following orders had been received; From position 35°41'N, 23°16'E about 1100 B/14 proceed dived and on the surface at 9 knots as requisite capable to rendezvous with the escort in position 35°49'N, 15°18'E at 0915 B/16. (74)

14 Jul 1944 HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) made the westbound passage through the AntiKithera Channel. Just before diving a southbound caique was sighted 3 miles to the westward but the vessel was so small that it was not considered worth to molest it. (7)
16 Jul 1944 At 1200 B/16, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) ended her 16th war patrol (15th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
This had been her last war patrol. She was now to return to the U.K. to refit. (7)
This convoy departed Port Said around 1500 C (1200 Z = G.M.T.) on 24 July 1944.

On departure from Port Said the convoy was made up of the following merchant vessels; Alphard (Dutch, 5483 GRT, built 1937), British Soldier (British (tanker), 5564 GRT, built 1918), Fernplant (British, 5274 GRT, built 1939), Fort Erie (British, 7128 GRT, built 1943), Francisco Coronado (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), George L. Baker (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), George Taylor (American, 7181 GRT, built 1942), J. Frank Cooper (American, 7207 GRT, built 1944), John A. Donald (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), John Ireland (American, 7247 GRT, built 1944), Kong Sverre (Norwegian, 7238 GRT, built 1941), Lawrence D. Tyson (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), Lidvard (Norwegian, 4785 GRT, built 1939), Luminetta (British (tanker), 6159 GRT, built 1927), Polartank (Norwegian (tanker), 6356 GRT, built 1930), Samblade (British, 7219 GRT, built 1943), Samglory (British, 7210 GRT, built 1944), Samkey (British, 7219 GRT, built 1943), Samleyte (British, 7255 GRT, built 1944), Tricolor (Norwegian, 6850 GRT, built 1933) and William W. Mayo (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943).
On departure from Port Said the convoy was escorted by the sloop HMS Deptford (Lt.Cdr. H.R. White, RN) and the corvette HMS Campion (Lt. T.F. Alderman, RNR).

Around 1230 C/25, the following ships joined the convoy coming from Alexandria; Empire Brook (British, 2852 GRT, built 1941), Empire Lionel (British, 7030 GRT, built 1942), Fort Liard (British, 7133 GRT, built 1942) and Samfleet (British, 7210 GRT, built 1944).
The Fleet Tender (coastal transport) FT 16 was also part of the convoy.
They were escorted by the sloop HMS Amethyst (?), minesweeping sloop Commandant Dominé (?) and the A/S whaler HMSAS Southern Maid (?).
The Samleyte was detached to Alexandria with engine defects.
Around 0730 B/29, the Augusta joiners came in sight.

Around 0845 B/29, in position 36°22'N, 15°15'E, the following merchant vessels were detached to Augusta; Empire Lionel, Fort Liard, Luminetta, Polartank, Samblade, Samfleet, Samglory, Samkey and Tricolor.

The following merchant vessel coming from Augusta then joined the convoy; Charles Piez (American, 7191 GRT, built 1943), Dallington Court (British, 6889 GRT, built 1929), Emma Willard (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), Horace Mann (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942), Jacob H. Gallinger (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), John Dockweiler (American, 7207 GRT, built 1944), John Murray Forbes (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942), Joseph Holt (American, 7181 GRT, built 1942), Malancha (British, 8124 GRT, built 1937, Palermo (British, 2797 GRT, built 1938), Sirocco (American, 8258 GRT, built 1944), Sturdy Beggar (American, 8271 GRT, built 1944), Taria (Dutch (tanker), 10354 GRT, built 1939), William L. Yancey (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943) and William Moultrie (American, 7177 GRT, built 1942).
Around 1400 B/29, in position 36°30'N, 14°22'E, FT 12 was detached to Malta.

Around 1500 B/29 the merchant vessel James M. Gillis (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943) joined the convoy coming from Malta. Also joining from Malta was the Royal Fleet Auxiliary tanker Celerol (2649 GRT, built 1917). Besides these vessels the submarines HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN), HMS Unsparing (Lt. A.D. Piper, DSC and 2 bars, RNR) and the motor minesweeper HMS BYMS 2028 (A/Skr.Lt. J.R. Clark, RNR) also joined.

Around 1500 B/30, in position 37°21'N, 09°56'E, the following ships were detached to Bizerta; Alphard, British Soldier, Dallington Court (originally bound for Algiers but straggled and was ordered to proceed to Bizerta), Empire Brook, Horace Mann and J. Frank Cooper. The RFA tanker Celerol was also detached.
With them the British escort vessels, HMS Deptford, HMS Amethyst, HMS Campion and HMSAS Southern Maid parted company.
Commandant Dominé remained with the convoy as her destination was Algiers.

At the same time the the new American escort made up of the cutter USCGC Campbell (Cdr. S.F. Gray, USCG, with COMTASKFOR 61, T/Capt. J.C. Sowell, USN on board) and the destroyer escorts USS Evarts (Lt.Cdr. F.A. Harding, Jr., USNR, with COMCORTDI5 5, Cdr. R.A. Fitch, USNR on board), USS Wyffels (Lt. S.N. Gleis, USNR), USS Decker (Lt.Cdr. H.S. Cody, Jr., USNR), USS Dobler (Lt.Cdr. E.F. Butler, USNR), USS Walter S. Brown (Lt. L.C. Burdett, USNR), USS Gillette (Lt.Cdr. J.W. Daniel, Jr. USNR, with COMCORTDIV 56, T/Cdr. W.L. Harmon, USN on board), USS Henry R. Kenyon (Lt.Cdr. A.D. Berliss, Jr., USNR), USS Gunason (Cdr. H.G. White, USNR), USS Major (Lt.Cdr. O.W. Goepner, USNR) and USS Weeden (Lt.Cdr. C.F. Tillinghast, Jr., USNR) joined. These vessel had been exercises off Bizerta since around 0700 B/29.

Around 1600 B/30, the following merchant vessels joined the convoy coming from Bizerta; British Endurance (British (tanker), 8406 GRT, built 1936), Gulfpoint (American (tanker), 6972 GRT, built 1920), Louis McLane (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942), Lucretia Mott (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), Prosper Schiaffino (French, 1634 GRT, built 1931), San Cipriano (British (tanker), 7966 GRT, built 1937), Tibia (Dutch (tanker), 10356 GRT, built 1939) and Topsdalfjord (Norwegian, 4271 GRT, built 1921).
The naval tanker USS Escalante (Cdr. C.L. Kiewert, USNR) also joined the convoy.
Around 0400 B/31, the merchant vessel Lidvard was detached to Bone.
Around 2000 B/31, the merchant vessel John A. Donald was detached to Algiers to land the British Commodore there and then rejoin the convoy.

Around 0650 B/1, two escorts, USS Weeden and, most likely, USS Major, were detached to Algiers with the Algiers section of the convoy which was made up of the following merchant vessels; Prosper Schiaffino and San Cipriano.
At 0655 B/1, the Commandant Domine was also detached to Algiers.

Around 0820 B/1, the following merchant vessels joined the convoy coming from Algiers; British Power (British (tanker), 8451 GRT, built 1936), Finistere (French, 1158 GRT, built 1909), Joseph H. Nicholson (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), Pan-Maryland (American (tanker), 7701 GRT, built 1938), Robert L. Hague (American, 7207 GRT, built 1944), Silvester Gardiner (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), Stephen A. Douglas (American, 7219 GRT, built 1942), Thomas B. Reed (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943) and Walter E. Ranger (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943).
Also the John A. Donald and the two escort vessels rejoined the convoy.

Around 0930 B/1, the British Power broke down temporarily. USS Decker was ordered to assist / escort the stricken vessel. After repairs they were able to rejoin the convoy which they did around 0335 B/2.
Around 2105 B/1, the destroyer escort
USS Smartt (Lt. E.R. Wepman, USNR) joined the convoy coming from Oran.
Around 0615 B/2, USS Major and USS Weeden were ordered to proceed ahead and contact the joiners from Oran and escort them to the rendezvous.
Around 0625 B/2, the following merchant vessels were detached to Oran; Joseph H. Nicholson, Louis McLane, Lucretia Mott, Palermo, Thomas B. Reed and William W. Mayo.

Around 0805 B/2, the Oran joiners commenced taking their assigned stations in the convoy. The following merchant vessels joined the convoy coming from Oran; Carrillo (American, 5013 GRT, built 1911), Egbert Benson (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942), F.A.C. Muhlenberg (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942), Francis L. Lee (American, 7191 GRT, built 1943), Haym Salomon (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), Isaac Sharpless (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), James Manning (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), John H. Eaton (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), Jonathan Worth (American, 7177 GRT, built 1943), Joshua Seney (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942), Poza Rica (Italian (tanker), 7799 GRT, built 1940) and Robert M.T. Hunter (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943).
Also joining from Oran were the naval tug USS Abnaki (T/Lt. D. Walley, USN) which had the damaged French destroyer escort Sénégalais (?) in tow.
At 1030 B/3, the merchant vessel Finistere was detached to Gibraltar as were HMS Unruly, HMS Unsparing and HMS BYMS 2028.
The Norwegian tanker Gard (Norwegian (tanker), 8259 GRT, built 1938) joined the convoy coming from Gibraltar.

Around 1800 B/3, the following merchant vessels joined the convoy coming from Casablanca; Calvin Coolidge (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), Cape Brenton (British, 6044 GRT, built 1940), Cochrane (British, 7203 GRT, built 1923), Empire Garrick (British (tanker), 8128 GRT, built 1942), Franz Klasen (Panamanian (tanker), 12425 GRT, built 1932), John Barton Payne (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943) and Samshire (British, 7219 GRT, built 1943).

These vessels had been escorted to the rendezvous by the French patrol vessels Le Ruse, L'Emporté and L'Effronté which now to the merchant vessels with them which were to proceed to Casablanca. This were the following; Egbert Benson and Topsdalfjord. They arrived at Casablanca on 4 August.
At 0118 N/6, the Empire Garrick broke down. USS Decker was ordered to stand by the stricken vessel. They rejoined the concoy at 1350 N/6.
At 0900 N/7, escorts commenced refuelling from USS Escalante.
At 1330 O/7, the Samshire was detached to Angra do Heroísmo, Terceira, Azores. The A/S trawler HMS Mazurka (A/Skr.Lt. J. Baxter, RNR) had come from the Azores to escort her.
At 1434 O/7, fuelling operations were completed. All twelve escorts were fuelled on this day.

At 0444 O/8, the destroyer escort USS Underhill (Lt.Cdr. S.R. Jackson, USNR) joined the convoy having completed her repairs at Algiers after which she sailed to overtake the convoy. She refuelled from USS Escalante later that morning from 0900 O/8 to 0950 O8.
At 0838 P/9, the Isaac Sharpless broke down. USS Decker was ordered to stand by the stricken vessel. They rejoined the convoy at 1140 P/9.
At 1645 P/9, the Empire Garrick reported fuel pump trouble and was unable to keep up. She rejoined the convoy at 2045 P/9.
At 1655 P/9, the Sirocco broke down and dropped out of the convoy. She rejoined the convoy at 1905 P/9.
At 0610 P/13, the Poza Rica suffered steering trouble and dropped out of the convoy. She rejoined the convoy at 0742 P/13.
At 0615 P/13, escorts commenced refuelling from USS Escalante.
At 0815 P/13, the Poza Rica suffered steering trouble and dropped out of the convoy. She rejoined the convoy at 1715 Q/13. USS Decker had been detached to stand by the stricken vessel.
At 1230 Q/13, fuelling operations were completed. All thirteen escorts were fuelled on this day.
At 1845 Q/13, the Sirocco and Sturdy Beggar were detached to proceed independently to New York.
At 0545 Q/14, the Cape Brenton was unable to keep up due to bad coal. She was able to regain her assigned station at 1145 Q/14.

At 0730 Q/15, USS Escalante was detached to Bermuda. To escort her there the destroyer escorts USS Dufilho (Lt.Cdr. A.H. Nienau, USNR) and USS Kenneth M. Willett (Lt.Cdr. W.T. Flynn, USNR) had joined the tanker.
At 1700 Q/15, the Empire Garrick was detached to proceed to New York. USS Underhill went with her as escort.
At 0315 Q/16, the Jacob H. Gallinger suffered a hot bearing and needed to slow down. USS Decker was ordered to stand by. They rejoined the convoy at 1140 Q/16.
At 0900 Q/16, the destroyer escort USS Rudderow Cdr. M.W. Greenough, USNR) joined coming from Norfolk, Virginia.
At 0905 Q/16, USS Rudderow parted company taking USS Abnaki and her tow, Sénégalais with her. They were to proceed to Charleston, South Carolina.

At 1525 Q/16, the convoy split. The Chesapeake Bay section proceeded to it's destination escorted by Escort Division 5 made up of USS Evarts, USS Wyffels, USS Decker, USS Dobler and USS Walter S. Brown.
The Chesapeake Bay section was released off it's destination around 2300 Q/17 after which the escorts set course for the Boston Navy Yard.
The remainder of the convoy proceeded by New York with the remaining escorts.
At 0700 Q/17, the Taria parted company with the New York section to proceed to Philadelphia, Pennsylvania taking the USS Gunason with her as escort.
Around 2200 Q/17, the New York section arrived at it's destination. The remaining escorts proceeded to the Boston Navy Yard via the Cape Cod Canal. (77)

29 Jul 1944 Around 0800 B/29, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) departed Malta for Gibraltar. She made the passage together with HMS Unsparing (Lt. A.D. Piper, DSC and 2 bars, RNR). The submarines joined convoy GUS 47 for the passage to Gibraltar.
[As no logs are available for this period no map can be displayed for the passage back to the U.K.]
[For more info on the convoy see the 'event', 'Convoy GUS 47' for 24 July 1944.] (78)
This convoy departed Freetown on 30 July 1944.

On departure from Freetown the convoy was made up of the following merchant vessels; Bosphorus (Norwegian, 2111 GRT, built 1934), Bothnia (British, 2407 GRT, built 1928), Calgary (British, 7206 GRT, built 1921), Fauzon (French, 4376 GRT, built 1938), Fort Cadotte (British, 7128 GRT, built 1943), Fort de Troyon (French, 5131 GRT, built 1919), Fort Panmure (French, 7155 GRT, built 1943), Fort Paskoyac (British, 7134 GRT, built 1942), Greystoke Castle (British, 5853 GRT, built 1928), Henri Jaspar (Belgian, 5760 GRT, built 1929), Ittersum (Dutch, 5199 GRT, built 1938), Northleigh (British, 5450 GRT, built 1937), Norwegian (British, 6366 GRT, built 1921), Wearwood (British, 4597 GRT, built 1930), Yearby (British, 5666 GRT, built 1929) and Zypenberg (Dutch, 4973 GRT, built 1920).

On departure from Freetown the convoy was escorted by the sloop HMS Enchantress (Lt.Cdr. E.D.J. Abbot, DSC, RN), frigate HMS Ness (T/A/Lt.Cdr. R.H. Marchington, MBE, RNVR) and the patrol vessel HMS Kilbirnie (T/Lt. E.R.H. Seddon, RNVR).

At 0800 Z/2, in position 14°44'N, 18°00'W, the following merchant vessels joined the convoy coming from Dakar; John Laurance (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942) and Marrakech (French, 6179 GRT, built 1914).
At 0945 Z/4, in position 21.14'N, 18.01'W, the John Laurance parted company to proceed to Port Etienne to due to one of the crew being seriously ill.
At 0900 Z/10, in position 35°15'N, 10°28'W, the Fauzan and Marrakech were detached to Casablanca.

Around 1030 Z/10, in position 35°18'N, 10°28'W, the following merchant vessels joined the convoy coming from Casablanca; Cap Cantin (British (former French), 3317 GRT, built 1933), Fort Ellice (British, 7129 GRT, built 1942) and Porjus (Swedish, 2965 GRT, built 1906).

These ships were escorted from Casablanca to the rendezvous by the minesweeping sloop Gazelle (?) and the patrol vessel L'Effronté (?) which subsequently escorted the two merchant vessels that had parted company with the convoy to Casablanca where they arrived on the 11th.
At the same time as the Casablanca section joined up convoy MKS 57(G) merged with convoy SL 166 into the combined convoy SL 166 / MKS 57(G).

The following merchant vessels made up convoy MKS 57(G) which now thus joined; Baron Douglas (British, 3899 GRT, built 1932), City of Dundee (British, 5273 GRT, built 1921), Clan MacNeil (British, 6111 GRT, built 1922), Cydonia (British, 3517 GRT, built 1927), Edward Bruce (British, 7219 GRT, built 1943), Empire Torrent (British, 7076 GRT, built 1942), Fort Prudhomme (British, 7167 GRT, built 1943), Manchester Division (British, 6048 GRT, built 1918), Marpessa (Dutch (tanker, serving as escort oiler), 7408 GRT, built 1927), Marsdale (British, 4890 GRT, built 1940), Nurani (British, 5414 GRT, built 1941), Ocean Pelgrim (American, 7177 GRT, built 1942), Pierre Soule (American, 7191 GRT, built 1943), Sarpedon (British, 11321 GRT, built 1923) and Sheaf Crown (British, 4868 GRT, built 1929). With these merchant vessels the rescue ship Goodwin (British, 1570 GRT, built 1917) and the submarines HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) and HMS Unsparing (Lt. A.D. Piper, DSC and 2 bars, RNR) also joined the convoy.
The escort of convoy KMS 57(G) took over the escort of the now combined convoy and HMS Enchantress and HMS Ness proceeded to Gibraltar arriving there on the 11th.

The new escort was made up of the frigates HMS Helmsdale (Cdr. C.W. McMullen, DSC, RN), HMS Foley (A/Lt.Cdr. C.A.H. Bird, RNVR) and the corvettes HMS Kenilworth Castle (Lt. J.J.Allon, RNR), HMS Launceston Castle (Lt. R.M. Roberts, RNR), HMS Pevensey Castle (A/Lt.Cdr. C.W. Leadbetter, RNR) and HMS Portchester Castle (Lt. A.G. Scott, RNR).
At the time the convoys merged the destroyer HMS Highlander (Cdr. D.A. Rayner, DSC, VRD, RNVR) also joined the convoy escort.

At 1050 Z/10, in position 35°18'N, 10°28'W, the Norwegian was detached by the Commodore to proceed to Casablanca due to a defective boiler. HMS Kilbirnie was given the task to escort her there. They arrived at Casablanca on the 11th.
At 1000 Z/11, in position 36°00'N, 14°18'W, the escort carrier HMS Campania (A/Capt. K.A. Short, RN) joined the combined convoy coming from the combined convoy OS 85 / KMS 59.
At 1600 Z/11, in position 36°17'N, 15°23'W, the frigate HMS Bayntun (Lt.Cdr. L.P. Bourke, RNZNR) joined the convoy.
At 1400 Z/12, in position 38°38'N, 18°00'W, the destroyer HMS Antelope (Cdr. J.G. Gould, RN) joined the convoy.
At 2000 Z/15, in position 48°52'N, 18°20'W, the Marpesa parted company with the convoy to join convoy OS 86.

At 0730 Z/16, in position 50°20'N, 17°54'W, the following merchant vessels of the convoy parted company to proceed ahead to the U.K. as fast section; Bosphorus, Greystoke Castle, Henri Jaspar and Marsdale. They took HMS Campania, HMS Antelope, HMS Foley and HMS HMS Portchester Castle with them as escorts. The fast section was dispersed at 0230 B/18 in the North Channel.
At 0700 B/18, HMS Helmsdale, HMS Kenilworth Castle, HMS Launceston Castle and HMS Pevensey Castle were detached to Londonderry where they arrived later the same day.
At 0740 B/18, the following merchant vessels were detached to proceed towards the North Channel. This were the Clyde, Mersey, Belfast and Bristol Channel sections of the convoy.
The Clyde section was made up of the following merchant vessels; City of Dundee, Cydonia, Pierre Soule and Zypenberg. The rescue ship Goodwin also went to the Clyde with them.
The Mersey section was made up of the following merchant vessels; Bothnia, Calgary, Clan MacNeil, Edward Bruce, Empire Torrent, Fort Cadotte, Fort Panmure and Ittersum.
The Belfast section was made up of only one merchant vessel, the Porjus.
The Bristol Channel section was made up of the following merchant vessels; Fort Ellice and Ocean Pilgrim.

HMS Unruly and HMS Unsparing also parted company to proceed with the North Channel section. At 1615 B/18, they parted company with the North Channel section to proceed to Dundee escorted by HMS Cutty Sark (Lt. H.J. Bartlett, DSC, RN).
The North Channel section was escorted by HMS Highlander which arrived at Londonderry on the 19th.

At 1200 B/19, the following merchant vessels arrived at Loch Ewe for onward routing to east coast ports; Baron Douglas, Cap Cantin, Fort de Troyon, Fort Paskoyac, Fort Prudhomme, Manchester Division, Northleigh, Nurani, Sarpedon, Sheaf Crown, Wearwood and Yearby. They were escorted to Loch Ewe by HMS Bayntun which subsequently proceeded to Londonderry where she arrived later on 19 August. (79)
3 Aug 1944 Around 1200 B/3, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) arrived at Gibraltar. (78)

8 Aug 1944 Around 1700 B/8, HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) departed Gibraltar for the U.K. She made the passage together with HMS Unsparing (Lt. A.D. Piper, DSC and 2 bars, RNR) in convoy MKS 57(G) which later joined convoy SL 166 coming from Gibraltar forming the combined convoy SL 166 / MKS 57(G).
[For more info on convoy MKS 57(G) see the 'event', 'Convoy MKS 57(G)' for 8 August 1944.]
[For more info on the combined convoy SL 166 / MKS 57(G) see the 'event' 'Convoy SL 166 / Combined convoy SL 166 / KMS 57(G)' for 30 July 1944.] (7)
The remaining merchant vessels of convoy MKS 57 passed Gibraltar into the Atlantic in the evening of 8 August 1944.

This were the following merchant vessels; City of Dundee (British, 5273 GRT, built 1921), Clan MacNeil (British, 6111 GRT, built 1922), Edward Bruce (British, 7219 GRT, built 1943), Fort Prudhomme (British, 7167 GRT, built 1943), Manchester Division (British, 6048 GRT, built 1918), Marsdale (British, 4890 GRT, built 1940), Nurani (British, 5414 GRT, built 1941), Pierre Soule (American, 7191 GRT, built 1943), Sarpedon (British, 11321 GRT, built 1923) and Sheaf Crown (British, 4868 GRT, built 1929).

The new escort for convoy MKS 57(G) had joined the convoy at 1900 B/8 relieving the Mediterranean escort which parted company to proceed to Gibraltar shortly afterwards. The new escort was made up of the following vessels; frigates HMS Helmsdale (Cdr. C.W. McMullen, DSC, RN), HMS Foley (A/Lt.Cdr. C.A.H. Bird, RNVR) and the corvettes HMS Kenilworth Castle (Lt. J.J.Allon, RNR), HMS Launceston Castle (Lt. R.M. Roberts, RNR), HMS Pevensey Castle (A/Lt.Cdr. C.W. Leadbetter, RNR) and HMS Portchester Castle (Lt. A.G. Scott, RNR).

At 2000 B/8, the following merchant vessels joined the convoy coming from Gibraltar; Baron Douglas (British, 3899 GRT, built 1932), Cydonia (British, 3517 GRT, built 1927), Durenda (British, 7241 GRT, built 1922), Empire Torrent (British, 7076 GRT, built 1942), Marpessa (Dutch (tanker, serving as escort oiler), 7408 GRT, built 1927) and Ocean Pelgrim (American, 7177 GRT, built 1942). The submarines HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN) and HMS Unsparing (Lt. A.D. Piper, DSC and 2 bars, RNR) also joined at this time for passage to the U.K. Also joining at this time was the rescue ship Goodwin (British, 1570 GRT, built 1917).
At 1305 B/9, the Durenda straggled from the convoy and returned to Gibraltar.
At 1030 Z/10, the convoy made rendezvous with convoy SL 166 coming from Freetown and the combined convoy SL 166 / MKS 57(G) was formed. (80)

24 Jul 1945 Around 0735 A/24, HMS Unruly (Lt. D. Hay, RN) arrived at Gosport (Portsmouth) from Rothesay.
At Gosport she was reduced to reserve in late July / early August. (91)
11 Sep 1945 HMS Unruly (Lt. L.D. Hamlyn, RN) departed Portsmouth for Lisahally. (78)
14 Sep 1945 HMS Unruly (Lt. L.D. Hamlyn, RN) arrived at Lisahaally from Portsmouth. Being still in reserve, she was laid up at Lisahally / Londonderry. (78)
ADM numbers indicate documents at the British National Archives at Kew, London.
